coredata.dc:description: Carbon has played a pivotal role in human civilization’s advancement, owing to its ability to form strong bonds in various structures. Over the past three decades, numerous carbon-based nanoparticles (CBNs) have been developed through diverse synthesis processes. CBNs have been utilized in a wide range of applications, including biomedicine, catalysis, environmental remediation, and textiles. This paper provides a comprehensive study of the CBNs. The first section includes detailed descriptions of fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, graphene nanoparticles, nanodiamonds, and carbon quantum dots and their properties. Also, It provides a comprehensive overview of the different synthesis methods. The third section of this paper shows the techniques used in the characterization process and the limitations of each one. The paper also supplies cross-disciplinary applications, including drug delivery, bioimaging, biosensing, catalysis, environmental remediation, and E-textiles. The study aims to offer a thorough evaluation of these materials, which will be valuable for future developments and broaden their range of applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon has played a pivotal role in human civilization’s advancement, owing to its ability to form strong bonds in various structures. Over the past three decades, numerous carbon-based nanoparticles (CBNs) have been developed through diverse synthesis processes. CBNs have been utilized in a wide range of applications, including biomedicine, catalysis, environmental remediation, and textiles. This paper provides a comprehensive study of the CBNs. The first section includes detailed descriptions of fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, graphene nanoparticles, nanodiamonds, and carbon quantum dots and their properties. Also, It provides a comprehensive overview of the different synthesis methods. The third section of this paper shows the techniques used in the characterization process and the limitations of each one. The paper also supplies cross-disciplinary applications, including drug delivery, bioimaging, biosensing, catalysis, environmental remediation, and E-textiles. The study aims to offer a thorough evaluation of these materials, which will be valuable for future developments and broaden their range of applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanotechnology has been a subject of research since the turn of the century, with significant advancements since Richard P. Feynman introduced the term in 1959 [1]. Nanoparticles (NPs) are a diverse group of materials characterized by having at least one dimension smaller than 100 nm, and their physiochemical characteristics, such as optical qualities, have been studied. The typical hues of 20 nm nanoparticles are yellowish gray for platinum (Pt), wine red for gold (Au), pitch black for palladium (Pd) and black for silver (Ag). These nanoparticles display distinct colors and characteristics, making them useful for bioimaging applications [2]. Variations in nanoshell thickness, aspect ratio, and NPs concentration cause the color of the solution to change, affecting the NPs' absorption characteristics. NPs consist of three distinct layers: the surface, composed of metal ions, polymers, surfactants, and functionalizable small molecules; the core, forming the central part of the nanoparticle; and the shell layer, which is chemically distinct from the core [3].

NPs are categorized based on chemical properties, morphology and size. Based on their physical and chemical characteristics some well-known classes of nanoparticles (NPs) are, carbon-based NPs, which fullerenes and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are two major types, metal NPs that are made from metal precursors and known for their localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) properties, ceramic NPs, which are inorganic nonmetallic solids produced through heating and cooling processes, available in amorphous, polycrystalline, dense, porous, or hollow forms, semiconductor NPs and polymeric NPs.

CBNs encompass various nanomaterials distinguished by their distinctive properties and extensive potential applications, notably fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, and graphene. These materials have undergone thorough examination for their prospective roles across diverse fields such as nanotechnology, biomedicine, and electronics [4–7]. Over the past 30 years, the evolution of CBNs has been quite fascinating. It started in the early 1990 s with the groundbreaking discovery of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) by Iijima in 1991. CNTs, with their unique tubular structure and extraordinary mechanical and electrical properties, quickly attracted attention for their potential applications in electronics, materials science, and nanotechnology [8]. Afterwards, specifically in 1993, the discovery of fullerenes (Buckyballs) expanded the scope of CBNs, offering a new form of carbon that also demonstrated remarkable properties [9]. These discoveries laid the groundwork for exploring carbon-based materials in various high-tech fields.

In 2004, researchers Andre Geim and Konstantin Novoselov at the University of Manchester successfully isolated a single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice called graphene [10]. The discovery of graphene is considered a significant shift in nanomaterials research, as graphene’s unique properties—such as remarkable electrical conductivity, strength, and flexibility—swiftly established it as a material of significant interest for numerous applications, ranging from electronics to energy storage. During this period, CNTs were also extensively studied for their potential in electronic devices, particularly in the development of field-effect transistors (FETs), which could enable faster, smaller, and more efficient electronic components [11]. By 2007, CNTs found their first real-world applications in energy storage technologies, particularly in enhancing the performance of lithium-ion batteries[12].

By the 2010 s, the applications of carbon-based nanomaterials began to grow, with significant advances in their use for energy storage, electronics, and water filtration. In 2010, graphene oxide was explored for water filtration applications due to its large surface area and capability to filter small particles and even salts. During the same period, graphene-based supercapacitors were developed, providing new opportunities for high-capacity, rapid-charging energy storage devices. By 2011, both CNTs and graphene began being utilized in flexible electronics, leading to the development of wearable devices and new forms of quantum computing research. Additionally, CNTs found applications in reinforced materials, such as lightweight yet strong composites used in automotive and aerospace industries, where their strength-to-weight ratio provided significant benefits [13,14].

In the mid-2010 s, graphene-based supercapacitors became commercially available. These supercapacitors were praised for their capability to store significant amounts of energy and charge more quickly than traditional batteries. By 2017, carbon-based nanomaterials, particularly CNTs and graphene oxide, were gaining popularity in biomedical applications. Researchers began exploring these materials for drug delivery systems and biosensors thanks to their biocompatibility, high surface area, and the potential for functionalization to facilitate specific biological interactions[15]. Additionally, graphene and CNTs were widely used in smart textiles and wearable electronics, leading to the development of fabrics embedded with integrated sensors and energy storage systems[16].

In the 2020 s, research and applications of carbon-based nanomaterials have expanded into new areas. Carbon nanomaterials are now utilized in environmental remediation, especially for CO2 capture and water purification technologies. These materials are designed to tackle environmental challenges by capturing harmful gases and purifying contaminated water[17,18]. By 2023, researchers had developed graphene-based sensors for real-time detection of biological pathogens, which offer promising applications in healthcare, diagnostics, and environmental monitoring [19,20]. Fig. 1 shows the timeline for the research and utilization of CBNs in various applications.

The exceptional properties of CBNs, such as their high surface area, thermal and mechanical stability, electrical conductivity, inorganic semiconducting attributes, and organic π-π stacking features, enable efficient interaction with biomolecules and enhanced light responses [21]. CBNs also possess excellent field emission properties, with a low threshold field and high emission current density, making them attractive for technological applications [22]. In addition, CBNs have been found to have promising prospects in energy-related applications, such as in electronics, batteries, and wastewater treatment [23]. Furthermore, CBNs have demonstrated notable attributes such as high specific surface area, electrical conductivity, carrier mobility, optical transparency and flexibility, rendering them well-suited for a broad spectrum of applications [5,24]. However, the potential toxicity of these materials is a concern that needs to be addressed [5].

Carbon-based nanomaterials have seen significant progress in recent years due to their exceptional properties and diverse applications. A comprehensive review is crucial to consolidate current knowledge, identify gaps, and guide future research. This review would provide valuable insights into how materials like graphene, carbon nanotubes, and fullerenes perform across different sectors, helping to prioritize areas for further investigation and exploring new applications. Additionally, it would assess synthesis techniques, highlight challenges in scalability and reproducibility, and suggest improvements for translating lab-scale successes into industrial applications. Thus, this study aims to provide a consolidated review of carbon-based nanomaterials to understand the current state of research, optimize material performance, and ensure their safe, sustainable, and effective use across industries. This would help advance the development of these materials while addressing existing challenges and maximizing their potential.

CBNs are a diverse class of nanomaterials composed predominantly of carbon atoms, known for their unique properties and wide-ranging applications. These nanoparticles include fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, graphene, and nanodiamonds, each with distinct and unique structural features. Fig. 2 illustrates some unique properties of CBNs.

These carbon-based nanoparticles are utilized in various fields, including medicine for drug delivery systems, electronics for enhancing conductivity in composite materials, and environmental science for water purification and pollutant removal. Their versatility and functional properties make them critical components in advancing nanotechnology and developing innovative solutions across multiple industries.

In 1985, Kroto and colleagues noticed abnormal results in the mass spectra of the vaporized carbon samples. The results led to the discovery of fullerene [9]. Fullerene is the third carbon allotropic form after graphite and diamond. The simplest stable and the most commonly used fullerene is the C60 carbon allotrope, which involves 60 structurally equivalent sp2 hybridized carbon atoms arranged in an icosahedral symmetrically closed-cage structure with a diameter of 0.7 nm [25]. The C60 consists of 20 hexagons and 12 pentagons, where each atom has three bonds with 0.146 nm and 0.14 nm length for the single and double bonds, respectively [26]. A key concept for understanding fullerene stability is the Isolated Pentagon Rule (IPR), which states that the most stable fullerene structures typically avoid having adjacent pentagonal carbon rings. Instead, pentagons are isolated and surrounded by hexagons. This rule explains the geometric stability of many fullerene molecules and provides insight into their formation and synthesis[27,28]. The typical fullerene structure consists of fused hexagonal and pentagonal rings, forming spherical, ellipsoidal, or cylindrical shapes. The IPR plays a crucial role in determining the global arrangement of these rings. For instance, C60 adheres strictly to the IPR, with twelve pentagons positioned symmetrically around a spherical structure. Variations in this rule can result in different topologies, leading to fullerene molecules with distinct physical and chemical properties [29]. Carbon fullerenes are zero-dimensional structures, unlike graphite or diamond crystals, which have atomic arrangements [30]. The fullerene shape is identical to a building designed by the American architect Richard Buckminster Fuller. Therefore, fullerene is also anointed as buckminsterfullerene [9].

After five years of discovery, the synthesis of C60 was successfully achieved for the first time using the evaporation and condensation of graphite via the arc discharge method. In the arc discharge method, carbon clusters experienced annealing facilitated by a buffer gas flowing through a quartz tube, where the temperature range within the tube was 25 to 1000 °C. During the reaction, the carbon electrode on the negative side was consumed. Fullerenes were produced through the annealing of condensed carbon and collected in the water cooler trap. Chromatography could then be employed to isolate C60 from the mixture [31]. However, the detonation method was also used [32]. Later, scientists developed fullerene salts, open-cage fullerenes, and quasi fullerenes.

Fullerene (C60) has many desirable properties like excellent photo-optical and physicochemical properties, biocompatibility, high surface area and affinity for organic molecules [33,34]. Plus, these CBNs are known to penetrate a range of biological barriers which makes them suitable for drug delivery [35–38]. However, fullerene C60 is water insoluble, and therefore, modifications are needed that can improve its aqueous dispersibility and solubility. Its conjugation with certain modifiers can lead to a hike in its solubility [39–41]. Besides, some environmental conditions lead to the opening of one of the two (C = C) bonds present in the hexagons. Modifications can solve both of these problems. For example, fullerenol C60(OH)x), one of its derivatives, can be synthesized by introducing OH groups which have better solubility and lower toxicity [42,43]. The solubility of fullerenol depends on the number of OH groups. Nevertheless, it exhibits the highest stability for 12, 24, 36, or 48 OH groups. C60 experiences a reduction in toxicity by half when OH groups are introduced [43,44]. In fact, fullerenol has comparatively lower toxic effects on human skin (HDP) and liver carcinoma (HepG2) cells [43,44]. It is also found to have decreased toxic results on mouse L929 fibrosarcoma, rat C6 glioma, and U251 human glioma cell lines [43,45]. It is also prevalent from the fact that fullerenol causes apoptosis in cell lines, whereas induction of fullerene C60 causes necrosis. This difference in overall effect stems from the difference in the interaction of the nanomaterials with the intracellular metabolic pathways [43–45].

Apart from that, fullerene molecules can self-assemble to give rise to a number of higher-dimensional structures via different processes [46,47]. Nano-architectural structures, such as nano-whiskers, nanotubes, nanorods, nanosheets, and micro-cubes, are among such high-dimensional self-assemblies that are obtained through the liquid-liquid interface precipitation LLIP method [46,48,49]. Nano-whiskers (FNWs) are thin crystalline fibers. They are used in the construction of chemical sensors, solar cells, and photocatalysts [46,49]. Also, the application of FNWs to neural stem cells seems to boost their orientation and differentiation [46,50]. Whereas nanotubes (FNTs) are highly tensile cylindrical nanostructures that have high electrical conductivity. These properties allow their applications in the field of nanocomposites and nanoelectronics [46,51]. Moreover, FNTs have been found to improve ischemic heart tissue repair by elevating electrical conductivity in tissue engineering scaffolds [46,52]. Some forms of fullerene also show antibacterial properties. Production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), DNA and cell membrane damage, and protein degradation are some of the antibacterial pathways employed by C60 and C70 [53,54].

C60 is an outstanding electron acceptor due to its high electron affinity. On top of that, it has wonderful electron transport properties [55,56]. Fullerenes exhibit attracting electrochemical behavior, making them highly relevant for applications in energy storage, photovoltaics, and sensors. Fullerenes' ability to accept electrons allows them to participate in redox reactions, providing a platform for their use in organic solar cells and batteries. Research has shown that the electrochemical stability of fullerenes can be improved by modifying their structure, such as by adding functional groups or creating fullerene derivatives[57]. In this context, the aforementioned properties make C60 a suitable candidate for the preparation of organic photovoltaics and field-effect transistors [56,58,59]. As fullerene is photostable, relatively inert toward the environment and possesses broad-band visible light absorption, it is a better candidate for environmental applications compared to mainstream organic photosensitizers [60,61]. On the flip side, its application in water remediation can be limited since it forms nanoscale aggregates (nC60, nC70, etc.) on its dispersion in water which in turn affects its properties [61–65]. In the case of nC60 when molecules get in close proximity as a result of aggregation it leads to quenching of the excited states which results in insignificant photoactive behavior [61,66,67]. Fullerenes exhibit distinct magnetic properties, which depend on the size and nature of the fullerene molecule. The presence of unpaired electrons in fullerene derivatives, especially in charged or doped species, can give rise to paramagnetic behavior. Moreover, Some fullerene molecules display quantum effects, making them candidates for research in spintronic devices and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)[68].

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are 1-dimensional hollow cylinders made from rolling-up graphite sheets with exceptional inherent properties [69,70]. The tubular shell primarily consists of hexagonal rings of carbon atoms arranged in a sheet-like structure and the ends are capped by half-dome-shaped half-fullerene molecules [26]. The number of graphene layers determines the type of CNTs. Accordingly, a single layer of carbon atoms forms single-walled CNTs (SWCNTs), while the multi-concentrically interlinked layers make multi-walled CNTs (MWCNTs). The diameter of CNTs ranges from 0.2-2.5 nm for SWCNTs and a few nanometers to 100 nm for the MWCNTs. The length extends from nanometers to centimeters [71–73].

CNTs have emerged as one of the most extensively studied nanostructures in recent decades [74]. Although the discovery of CNTs remains partially ascribed, they have been present in scientific research since 1991, when Sumio Iijima published his findings [8]. This seminal work presented a methodology for acquiring cylindrical and concentric carbon structures, later termed MWCNTs. Subsequently, in 1993, Iijima and Donald Bethune independently published studies detailing methods to synthesize SWCNTs. Following these breakthroughs, nanotubes became a focal point of research across various scientific disciplines [75]. In this context, scientists have prepared CNTs via several approaches, including plasma-based, thermal preparation, and hydrothermal methods. Chemical vapor deposition (CVD) is one of the thermal methods that has been favored over others due to its ability to produce CNTs in different forms and the ease of monitoring CNTs' growth at distinct temperatures [25,76].

With their high specific surface area, exceptional mechanical strength, and electrochemical stability, CNTs are an appealing option for biomedical applications [77,78]. CNTs are easy to modify both covalently (direct or defect) and non-covalently (surfactant or polymer attachment) [78,79]. There has been a rise in the application of CNTs for drug delivery. It can be attributed to the fact they are inert which makes them biocompatible [80]. Moreover, they can deliver drugs with precision and targeted release [80]. CNTs’ size and morphology make them highly fluid and permeable which in turn allows them to deliver drugs, proteins and vaccines [81]. An advantage of loading drugs on CNTs is the prevention of drug degradation thanks to their surface properties which allow the loading of drugs both on their surface and inside [81–83]. However, pristine CNTs can be hard to dissolve and have low dispersibility in water due to aggregation of the CNT particles caused by van der Waals and hydrophobic interactions [81]. Their low dispersibility may result in toxicity. On top of that, aggregation can increase the overall size of the carrier which poses difficulty in permeating cells [79,81]. Functionalized CNTs are water soluble and can penetrate biological barriers without inflicting any toxicity [80,84,85].

SWCNTs have lower cytotoxicity compared to other CNTs [86,87]. They can interact noncovalently or covalently with drugs and biomolecules [87–90]. Besides, they can form strong π–π interactions with species containing aromatic groups [87,89,90]. Their binding is further improved in their functionalized forms. For example, 1D functionalized CNTs (f-CNTs) are flexible and thus can interact with molecules through multiple binding sites [87,91]. SWCNTs are highly photostable, and they can act as shortwave infrared (SWIR) emitters [92]. As a result, they have gained a huge interest in bioimaging and sensing [92–94]. They have quasi-1D structure with a highly delocalized π-electron network which gives rise to narrow and diameter-dependent optical bands, thus leaving room for the adjustment of diameter and chirality in a way that absorption and emission can be tuned in a particular fashion [92,95–99]. However, their application for single-particle deep-tissue imaging and tracking has limited scope because of their low fluorescence quantum yield (QY varies between 10–4 and 0.01) in aqueous suspensions [92,100–103]. These CNTs are promising candidates for a plethora of biological applications using NIR. In this regard, lf-SWCNTs, a modified version, is one step ahead of their unmodified counterpart. They come with low-density structural defects known as quantum defects or locally functionalized (lf) sites which ensures more intense NIR fluorescence at relatively longer wavelengths (>1000) [104–107]. These sites offer multiple advantages including brilliant fluorescence up to NIR-II, and easy-to-adjust wavelength range through modifications [105,107,108]. Moreover, they absorb faintly at emission wavelengths which prevents any significant quenching effect [107,109]. Unfortunately, lf-SWCNTs have their own drawbacks such as difficulty in dispersion in water due to their hydrophobic surfaces which results in clumping and ultimately toxicity [91,107]. Therefore, they need to be dispersed using some dispersant such as phospholipid-polyethylene glycol [107,110].

CNTs are also regarded as potential biosensing materials due to properties like large surface area, strong chemical reactivity, and high electrical conductivity [111,112]. Their large surface area gives them high sensitivity because analytes can come in contact with them at multiple sites. When a CNT-based sensor interacts with an analyte, it experiences a change in its conductance [112,113]. This analyte can bring about this change by changing the number of charge carriers [112,114]. The sensor output terminals measure this change which is used to identify the analyte and to determine its concentration. Despite the huge potential of these sensors, their application in functional and commercial devices is still limited due to the lack of proper understanding of how to fit them into these devices [112,115].

The structure of graphene is composed of a two-dimensional single layer of carbon atoms with sp2 hybridization arranged in a hexagonal crystalline structure [10,116]. In graphene, carbon atoms are bonded to each other by sigma (σ) bonds, where the bond length reaches 0.142 nm [117,118]. Every carbon atom within this lattice possesses a π-orbital, which contributes extensively to the delocalization of the electron network. This structural arrangement imparts remarkable stability to graphene compared to other nanosystems [119]. Even though the research about graphene started in 1947 by the Canadian physicist P. R. Wallace, graphene is the youngest carbon nanomaterial as the first sample was described in 2004 by A.Geim (Dutch-British physicist) and K. Novoselov (Russian-British physicist. Since that time, graphene has had the attention [118,120].

It is the thinnest material known to humanity and is used as a building block for various carbon-based materials. This versatile substance can be shaped into one-dimensional CNTs and arranged into three-dimensional graphite structures. When pentagons enter into its hexagonal lattice, graphene can even be curved into spherical fullerenes [26,121,122]. Graphene has extraordinary properties [123] such as expansive specific surface area (single layer reaches 2630 m2/g) [124] with superior mechanical stiffness and high elasticity (Young’s modulus is about 1100 GPa) [125], distinguished optical transmittance (the white light absorbance percentage approximately equals 2.3 %) [126], remarkably high electronic and thermal conductivities (6300 S/cm and 5000 W/m.K, respectively) [127], and impermeability to gases [128]. In addition to that, graphene has attracted workers in the water/wastewater treatment sector as graphene contains copious functional groups, strong negative charge, and enormously hydrophilic features [129,130]. As a result, graphene is the precursor to all graphitic materials and has the potential for diverse applications.

Many forms of graphene find applications in biomedicine due to a bundle of advantages rising from their distinct properties. For example, graphene oxide (GO) has better hydrophilicity and lower thickness compared to pristine graphene; thus, it is preferable for drug delivery [131,132]. Plus, GO has high adsorption capacity, colloidal stability and is biocompatible. The GO surface shows hydrophilic interactions and π-π stacking with anticancer drugs [132,133]. Moreover, it offers a large specific surface area and a variety of functional groups, such as epoxy, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups [132,134]. The large surface area of these CBNs provides a platform where drugs can interact with them which facilitates drug transportation. In addition, GO-based hydrogels are considered as a choice for drug delivery because they display a pH-sensitive drug release trend [132,135]. These hydrogels allow for controlled drug release as pH changes [132,136]. But GO suspensions are acidic in nature which has a deteriorating effect on hydrogel and can cause damage to the cells. This problem can be solved by using another version of graphene, namely GO graphene nanosheets (GNS).

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are also one of the graphene nanoparticles investigated for their applications in the field of drug delivery [137]. GQDs are chemically, mechanically and thermally stable. They are highly biocompatible and have low toxicity [138]. With all these distinct properties, they also stand as an appealing choice for drug delivery [138–141]. They can interact with hydrophobic anticancers and are stable in the body due to their very small lateral size monoatomic layer and the existence of oxygen-rich groups at their fringes [137]. GQDs also favor traceable drug delivery to cancer cells without any need for extra labelling thanks to quantum confinement effects and edge effects that give rise to fluorescence [137,142]. The majority of GQDs show fluorescence in the visible region, meanwhile, adjustments can be made to introduce electronic states from the functional groups and defect sites that cause fluorescence in near-infrared [143,144]. Biocompatible NIR-emissive GQDs are small, water-soluble and can effectively be extracted from the cells and organs which leads to very low in vitro and in vivo toxicity [144–147]. Different biomedical fields such as photodynamic therapy and bioimaging use NIR-absorbing GQDs-based materials [148,149]. Like other CBNs, GQDs also have enormous surface area. In addition, new phenomena li࡫e defect- and intrinsic-state emissions can take place due to the abundance of π–π conjugations and the groups on the surface [149,150]. Intrinsic-state emissions might involve localized electron-hole pair recombination, the quantum size effect and zigzag edge sites, while defect-state emission is attributed to energy traps [148].

Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) have become a fascinating group of materials because of their adjustable light-emitting characteristics, which span the visible and near-infrared (NIR) ranges[151]. The discovery of blue-emitting GQDs marked the beginning of the first phase in GQD research. Typically, methods such as oxidative cutting and hydrothermal treatment of graphene oxide (GO) were employed to produce these GQDs[152]. The blue emission was primarily attributed to electronic transitions within the GQDs, which were affected by the oxygen-containing groups on their surface, such as the π-π* (from the sp2 carbon domains), nO 2p-π* (associated with protonated –COO− groups), and nN 2p-π* (linked to the amino groups), along with the quantum confinement effect[153]. The strong blue fluorescence and exceptional biocompatibility of GQDs were emphasized in key studies, showcasing their potential for use in bioimaging and optoelectronic applications. As research progressed, the emphasis moved toward generating environmentally friendly emissions from graphene quantum dots (GQDs). This was accomplished by refining the synthesis methods to more precisely control the size, surface properties, and functional groups of the GQDs.The green emission from GQDs is primarily attributed to electronic transitions such as π-π* (from the sp2 carbon domains), nO 2p-π* (related to the protonation and deprotonation of –COO− groups), and mainly nN 2p-π* (arising from the amino groups). These transitions are influenced by functional groups like amino, carboxyl, and ammonium carboxylate on the GQD surface[154]. Additional processes such as chemical reduction and surface passivation were commonly applied to further tune the emission properties of green-emitting GQDs[155]. Shortly after the initial discovery of blue and green emissions, researchers also identified yellow and orange emissions from GQDs. These emissions were achieved through controlled synthesis, where specific functional groups and surface states—such as nitrogen doping—were incorporated into the GQDs to tune their optical properties[156]. Red emission from GQDs was discovered much later than blue and green emissions, with its development facing challenges due to the need for precise control over the structural and electronic properties of the dots[157]. In 2017, red-emitting GQDs (R-GQDs) were first reported, using o-phenylenediamine and catechol as precursors. Since then, high-quality red-emitting GQDs have been produced through various advanced techniques, including microwave-assisted synthesis, electrochemical exfoliation, and laser ablation. The red emission observed in these GQDs resulted from the presence of various nitrogen species, such as pyrrolic, pyridinic, and amino nitrogen, on their surface, which enabled triple fluorescence emissions. To achieve red emission, a smaller bandgap is necessary, which can be engineered by functionalizing the GQDs in specific ways while minimizing defects[158].

Graphene possesses high potential as a sensor because it has many suitable properties. Graphene is so sensitive to its surroundings that any surface modification leads to a change in its properties. Also, its hexagonal lattice arrangement of sp2–sp2 hybrid carbon atoms results in a high density of free π-electrons in its structure. Consequently, it can have direct interactions with other molecules. Moreover, it has exceptional electrical conductivity due to the presence of highly mobile charge carriers [159,160]. Additionally, since this nanomaterial has a very huge specific surface area, as mentioned earlier, it has very good electrochemical reactivity with electrolytes [160,161].

Graphene can act both as a catalyst and adsorbent which shows its great versatility in environmental applications. It offers a great deal of advantages in catalysis. For starters, it has an enormous surface area which is enough to provide a platform for nanoparticles involved in catalytic activity [162–167]. Even though graphene is chemically inert in nature, it is easy to modify by doping with heteroatoms, introducing functional groups or straining it mechanically which can make it catalytically active [162–169]. Besides, it is observed that wrinkled and strained areas of graphene are more favorable for surface functionalization compared to flat regions [167,170–172]. Single-layered graphene is highly opaque and shows distinct electrical characteristics. It is a potential photocatalyst because it has outstanding dispersibility in water and its bandgap can be tuned as required [173,174]. Few-layer graphene (FLG) is another form of graphene that is suitable for catalytic reactions [175–178]. There is an enormous number of high-energy sites on its surface and along the edges where catalytic reactions can take place [178–180]. GO, due to its enormous specific surface area and a huge number of oxygenated groups, can interact with a range of adsorbates and can act as an excellent adsorbent [181–183]. Apart from that, magnetic adsorbents of GO can be synthesized by introducing magnetic nanoparticles like Fe3O4. It makes the process of adsorption easier because such adsorbents don’t need filtration and centrifugation steps [183,184].

Diamond is the hardest natural material due to its crystal structure, which is pure sp3 hybrid. In diamonds, carbon atoms are bonded by strong covalent bonds, forming a tetrahedral symmetry. Diamond has a higher density than graphite (3.514 g/cm3) thanks to the flawless symmetric arrangement of the four valence electrons orbitals. The extraordinary strength of the diamond, along with its unique resistance to compression and incomparable hardness on both the Vickers and Mohs scales, is attributed to its densely packed crystalline structure. Moreover, the diamond's chemical stability is exceptional due to the properties of the C–C bonds, even in the presence of strong acids [185,186].

The introduction of the first nano-sized diamond particles occurred in the 1960 s, but it wasn't until the late 1960 s that they began to receive attention due to their various valuable properties [187,188]. Nanodiamonds (NDs) are formed from tetrahedrally bonded carbon nanoparticles that have crystal domains with a diamondoid-like topology and diameters of ∼ 10 nm [189]. The structure of NDs consists of several layers. The first layer, which contains 70–90 % carbon atoms, is similar to the diamond layer (sp3 bonded carbon with size of 2–3 nm). The first layer is covered by a second/intermediate layer with an onion-shaped carbon shell, which contains a coating of functional groups on its surface [190,191]. The intermediate layer of nanodiamonds (NDs) is characterized as a nonhomogeneous translational carbon shell, typically ranging from 0.4 to 1.0 nm in thickness [192]. The shell is formed from continuous onion-shaped carbon layers at its inner surface and graphitic carbon monolayers at its outer surface [193]. The sp2 /sp3 bonds in nanodiamonds (NDs) exhibit notable flexibility, enabling them to adopt two distinct geometrical forms i.e. (i) An amorphous shell characterized by a substantial presence of sp2 carbon atoms. (ii) A “Bucky diamond,” resembling a sheet with an sp2 graphene character, configured in the structure of a fullerene [194,195]. The intrinsic properties of NDs such as high surface areas, tunable surface structures, desirable mechanical and optical properties, rigidity, biocompatibility, conductivity, electrical resistance, and chemical stability have attracted attention [196,197].

Although carbon, phenols, pyrones, sulfonic acid, and carboxylic acid groups can be found on their surface, the presence of a small ratio of anhydrides, hydroxyl groups, and epoxide groups has also been noticed. Among these groups, carboxylic groups hold great potential as a starting point for modifications [198,199]. Owing to their chemical stability, NDs can maintain self-lubrication properties at temperatures as high as 1000 K [200–203]. They have garnered attention as additives in thermal management systems due to their efficient heat conduction originating from their small size and immense surface area to volume ratio. Such applications include their incorporation into electronics for heat dissipation purposes and lubricants for the transfer of heat energy [204,205]. They show a strong attachment to drugs and have good dispersibility in water due to their octahedral geometry [199,206]. Besides, nanodiamonds have natural impurities namely nitrogen impurities termed nitrogen-vacancy (N-V) which make them suitable for bioimaging [199,207].

Even though NDs are being explored for biomedical applications they suffer from some drawbacks. First of all, they aggregate on their introduction to the living environment because of their fast interaction with biomolecules like proteins [208–211]. Aggregation hinders the metabolic activity of the living system and causes imbalances in homeostasis. Secondly, it is prone to causing random interactions in serum components and blood vessels which ultimately leads to nonuniform blood circulation. One of the methods to prevent such interactions is to modify their surface with hydrophilic polymers which can produce a camouflage effect [212]. Moreover, their photocatalytic efficiency is limited because charge carriers generated with light are easily recombined. Preparation of composites with other materials is one of the ways to overcome this problem. For example, nanodiamond present in nanodiamond and graphene hybrids, experience adjustment in their bandgap width which leads to their photocatalytic activity under visible light [213–215].

Carbon dots are 0-dimensional nanoparticles with optical and electrical properties comparable to traditional quantum dots. However, unlike transition metal-based quantum dots, carbon dots are carbon-based, which makes them non-toxic and suitable for a broader range of applications. Carbon dots can be classified into two types based on their composition and synthesis: carbon quantum dots, which are derived from carbon precursor materials, and graphene quantum dots, which consist of a few small layers of graphene (typically < 20 nm). The specific composition of these dots significantly influences their electrochemical and fluorescent properties [216]. In this section, CQDs will be explained. For further information regarding GQDs, please refer to section 2.3.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were discovered accidentally when Xu and colleagues (2004) were separating and purifying single-wall carbon nanotubes [217]. Sun et al. (2006) coined the term “carbon quantum dots” for the fluorescent carbon nanoparticles. They introduced a synthetic method to enhance fluorescence emissions by surface passivation [218], which marked a significant milestone in CQD developments. The small-size carbon nanoparticles CQDs (less than 10 nm) are typically quasi-spherical nanoparticles characterized by amorphous to nanocrystalline cores. These cores consist predominantly of graphitic or turbostratic carbon (sp2 carbon), along with graphene and GO sheets fused by diamond-like sp3 hybridized carbon insertions [219,220].

CQDs have been recently studied to improve the fluorescence quantum yield. Distinct materials have been employed to achieve this objective. For instance, CQDs have been synthesized from apple juice [221], vegetables [222,223], natural polymers [224], and watermelon peels [225]. Also, the synthesis of CQDs has been accomplished via several methods like laser ablation [218], electrochemical oxidation [226], combustion/thermal microwave heating supported synthesis [227], chemical oxidation [228], hydrothermal carbonization [229], and pyrolysis [230]. Oxidized CQDs feature significant carboxyl moiety amounts on their surface. The oxygen content in oxidized CQDs varies between 5 to 50 % (by weight), depending on the specific synthetic route employed [219].

Over the past decade, a variety of methods for synthesizing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged, which can be broadly categorized into two main approaches: “Top-down” and “Bottom-up.” Each approach offers distinct pathways for creation and can be further refined through modifications during the synthesis process or subsequent treatments[231].

The top-down synthesis methods involve the breakdown and exfoliation of larger carbon materials, such as graphite, carbon fibers, carbon nanotubes, and coal. These methods typically utilize processes like oxidative cleavage, hydrothermal or solvothermal treatment, electrochemical reactions, and microwave and ultrasonic-assisted techniques. Each of these methods effectively reduces the size of the carbon source to yield quantum-sized particles with distinct optical properties[232].

In contrast, bottom-up approaches focus on the pyrolysis of small organic molecules—such as citric acid, glucose, fructose, and ascorbic acid—which can be used alone or in combination with other compounds to introduce heteroatoms like nitrogen, boron, or sulfur into the carbon dots (CQDs) [233]. These heteroatom-doped carbon dots exhibit enhanced functionality and properties, making them suitable for various applications. Furthermore, the surface of CQDs can be further modified during or after synthesis to tailor their electronic, optical, and chemical characteristics through techniques like heteroatom doping, functionalization, or surface passivation.

The top-down methods for synthesizing carbon dots (CDs) offer several notable advantages. Firstly, they enable the ability to scale production, making it easier to produce large quantities of CDs. Secondly, these methods typically result in well-defined structures with a high degree of control over the size and morphology of the CDs. While bottom-up techniques can be more challenging to control due to the potential formation of side products that require extra purification steps, they provide a unique set of benefits. These methods allow for easier surface modifications in a one-pot synthesis, and they can produce a wider variety of structures and functionalities compared to the top-down approach, which often results in CDs that closely mirror the structure of the initial precursor [234].

For the future commercialization of carbon dots, achieving mass production is essential to meet the demands of various applications [235]. Recently, bottom-up methods, particularly solvothermal synthesis, have gained popularity due to their low cost, relatively simple procedure, and use of non-toxic materials [236]. However, these methods are often limited in their yield and face challenges with purification, making them less suitable for large-scale production. To address this, Li et al. demonstrated that aldol condensation could be leveraged as a viable route for the large-scale synthesis of CDs, offering a more efficient process for industrial applications [157].

Biomass-derived precursors for synthesizing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) offer significant benefits for sustainable development[237]. Biomass, which includes plant materials, agricultural waste, and food by-products, is a renewable, abundant, and underutilized resource that can help address both environmental and economic challenges. Biomass is a renewable resource, unlike fossil fuels or synthetic chemicals, making it an ideal precursor for producing sustainable nanomaterials. Using agricultural waste (such as rice husks, orange peels, corn stalks, and wood chips) helps to reduce reliance on non-renewable carbon sources and minimizes waste [238]. Biomass utilization promotes waste valorization, turning unwanted by-products into high-value materials. The agricultural residues, often discarded or burned, can be transformed into valuable carbon quantum dots with unique properties for bioimaging, sensors, and energy applications. This approach supports a circular economy by reducing waste and creating economic opportunities. The synthesis of CQDs from biomass precursors typically requires lower energy input compared to traditional methods. This reduces greenhouse gas emissions and lowers the overall carbon footprint, aligning with global sustainability goals. Biomass-based processes typically use fewer toxic chemicals and produce fewer by-products, making them more environmentally friendly[239]. Also, carbon dots derived from biomass are often more biocompatible than those made from synthetic precursors, making them particularly suitable for biomedical applications such as drug delivery, bioimaging, and biosensing. The natural origin of biomass-derived CQDs can also reduce their toxicity, which is crucial for ensuring their safety in medical applications. Finally, biomass-based synthesis methods can positively impact the economy, particularly in rural and agricultural areas. This method can boost local economies and create jobs in green technologies and sustainable manufacturing processes by producing value-added products from waste materials[240].

CQDs exhibit a broad range of wavelength absorption span from 260 to 320 nm [241], along with size-dependent optical emission, high quantum yield for photoluminescence [242], low toxicity [243], tunable surface properties [244], and appropriate electron transfer characteristics [245]. These attributes, extensively elucidated by the Wang group, position CQDs as promising candidates for various applications. CQDs offer versatile functionalities in biomedicine [246], biosensors [247], solar cells [248], supercapacitors [249], and photocatalysts [250]. Moreover, recent explorations have extended their utility to bioimaging [251,252], drug delivery [253], and bone tissue engineering [188]. Additional applications encompass optoelectronics [254], biosensing [255], medicinal uses [256], and catalysis [257], highlighting the wide-ranging potential of CQDs and other carbon nanomaterials.

In contrast with traditional 1D or 2D carbon-based nanomaterials (carbon nanotubes and graphene etc), the 0D characteristic of CQDs offers them smaller and more uniform sizes, which promotes their dispersion in lubricants [258,259]. 0D CQDs also show a potential to act as nano-bearings i.e. they can replace sliding friction with rolling friction which in turn leads to the overall reduction in frictional forces [259,260]. CQDs have conjugated π-domains as well as surface defects that give them their fluorescent character. Strong UV absorption can occur due to transitions of electrons in conjugated π-domains but weak or no fluorescence. Migration of excitons to the energy trap can cause quenching via radiationless relaxation which results in weak emission. Meanwhile, electron-hole pair recombination in energy traps offered by surface defects produces strong fluorescence emission [245,261]. The optical properties of CQDs can be tuned with heteroatomic dopants. These dopants can bring changes in their band gap as well as electron density. Among heteroatomic dopants, nitrogen takes this modulation even a step ahead by producing energy traps in the band gap which can improve radiative transitions [262]. CQDs are capable of upconversion photoluminescence which extends their light absorption range [263,264]. On top of that, they can establish suitable redox conditions through effective charge separation caused by their ability to function as electron-donating agents [264,265]. In addition, they find their use in the fabrication of membranes and separation processes because their size and shape offer them appropriate dispersion in polar solvent systems as well as polymer matrices [266,267]. The properties of carbon-based materials are summarized in Table 1
.

The methodology described in this study is predicated upon the thermal breakdown of organic substances at elevated temperatures, resulting in the generation of carbon nanoparticles. This technique enables precise control of particle size and dispersion. It is appropriate for the synthesis of certain structures such as graphene and carbon nanotubes. One potential drawback is that this technology may need substantial energy inputs and incur significant costs owing to the requirement for precise temperature and pressure management [270].

Molten metal chloride solvents facilitate the decomposition of metal carbides, including Li2C2, NaHC2, and CaC2. This combination undergoes a reaction with transition metal halides, resulting in the formation of carbon nanoparticles, often in the form of nanotubes and fibers. The occurrence of these reactions takes place within a temperature range of 550 to 1000 degrees Celsius.

It involves the chemical breakdown of acetylene in saline solvents, facilitated by the presence of transition metal catalysts such as nickel, cobalt, and iron. The form of the carbon fibers produced is heavily influenced by the characteristics and content of the salt used throughout the manufacturing process. The aforementioned reactions are conducted within a temperature range of 550 to 1000 °C, whereas the most favorable outcomes in the creation of carbon fiber are seen at lower temperatures.

The implementation of these technologies involves the use of specialized reactors that enable meticulous regulation of reaction parameters, including temperature, gas flow rate, and pressure. The objective of these technologies is to generate carbon nanoparticles that possess precise characteristics and have the potential to be used in many technological applications, including ferromagnetic fluids [271] .

Gaseous carbon-containing compounds are introduced into a high-temperature chamber where they decompose and deposit carbon on a substrate. Even though this technique provides high control over particle properties, ideal for producing carbon nanotubes and graphene, it requires specialized equipment and can be expensive.

Fig. 3 illustrates the synthesis of CNTs through Chemical Vapor Deposition. CVD is a widely used technique for manufacturing carbon nanotubes and graphene with exceptional quality. The procedure involves the heat-induced decomposition of hydrocarbon or carbon-based gaseous substances in the presence of a metal catalyst. Carbon sources often used in various applications include methane, acetylene, and ethanol. The reaction often takes place on substrates such as silicon, quartz, or nickel, whereby the catalyst particles are used for deposition.

CVD is a technique used to produce aligned carbon nanotubes, graphene layers, and other structured carbon compounds [272,273]. These materials are crucial for electronics, sensors, and composite materials because they possess exceptional purity and can be shaped with precision [270].

The process involves harnessing the kinetic energy generated by milling balls inside a spinning cylinder to facilitate the fragmentation of bulk materials into nanoparticles. This technique includes many advantages such as being cost-effective, chemical-free, and appropriate for manufacturing graphene flakes, but the possibility of material contamination resulting from the milling medium, as well as challenges in achieving particle homogeneity can be few of its potential drawbacks [274].

This technique depends on the use of a high-energy laser to induce the vaporization of the desired substance, resulting in the formation of nanoparticles within a regulated setting. So, it enables the synthesis of nanoparticles with precisely specified characteristics, appropriate for carbon nanotubes of certain sizes. However the drawbacks associated with this approach are the need for specialized and expensive equipment, as well as a rather sluggish manufacturing rate [275,276].

This technique is widely used and considered a straightforward approach for the synthesis of carbon nanotubes and fullerenes. It entails the establishment of an electric arc between two carbon electrodes, which are positioned inside an inert gas environment, such as helium or argon. The high temperature produced by the arc induces the vaporization of carbon from one of the electrodes, subsequently leading to the condensation of carbon atoms and the formation of novel carbon structures [277,278].

This technique uses natural catalysts like vitamin C or plant extracts to reduce GO to graphene in an aqueous solution, which makes it environmentally friendly and avoids the use of harmful chemicals. However, conversion efficiency can be variable, and there may be a need for improvement to ensure quality and consistency [270].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis of luminescent carbon nanoparticles (Rt-CBNs) at room temperature
ce:section.ce:para: This synthesis is carried out through chemical oxidative polymerization of o-phenylenediamine (oPD) using ammonium persulfate as the oxidizer. The process involves chemical preparation, reaction, purification, and drying. The process is eco-friendly, reducing the use of hazardous chemicals and high-energy heating. It is cost-effective, using inexpensive raw materials and simple techniques. The luminescent nanoparticles produced can be used in environmental sensing, medical diagnosis, and optoelectronics. The properties of the nanoparticles can be easily controlled by adjusting polymerization conditions and chemical concentrations. This method offers a sustainable and practical solution for producing advanced luminescent carbon nanoparticles with potential applications across various technology and industrial sectors [279].

Even though the CBNs have a high potential for many applications, such as biomedicine, catalysis, the energy sector, and environmental applications, they need to be fully characterized to achieve optimum quality. It is important to identify the features of the nanomaterial, such as its shape, size, interparticle interaction, and surface structure. The properties of CBNs can be identified by distinct techniques. In this context, several techniques may be required to obtain the desired level of characterization.

Nanostructured materials and nanoparticles often exhibit optical characteristics that depend on concentration, size, and shape [280]. In UV–visible experiments, electromagnetic radiation ranging from 190 to 800 nm is employed to illuminate the sample. A UV–visible spectrometer measures the light intensity both before (I0) and after passing through (I) the sample, facilitating the determination of the absorbance (A) of the sample. The absorption of UV/visible radiation by a molecule induces electronic transitions between energy levels, commonly known as electronic spectroscopy [281].

Graphene and CNTs exhibit a strong absorption band near the wavelength of 230 nm. The strong absorptions of graphene and CNTs near the wavelength of 230 nm were ascribed to the π-π transition of aromatic C = C bonds [282,283]. Fascinatingly, graphene exhibits lower transparency in the UV–visible spectrum compared to GO, a trait ascribed to its lower concentration of sp2 carbons within its structure. This phenomenon was employed in reduction reaction monitoring and the assessment of the oxidization grade of GO. The transmittance was also found to decrease as the graphene layers increased [284]. Sun et al. (2010) utilized this feature to estimate the number of graphene layers via UV–visible absorbance at 550 nm [285]. UV–visible spectrophotometry was utilized to monitor the dispersion protocols of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), as an increase in absorbance intensity indicates enhanced dispersion. Furthermore, the concentration of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) in solutions can be measured using UV–visible absorbance [286–288]. UV–Vis spectroscopy has advantages over other techniques such as fast, simple, time-saving, easy to operate, and it has no calibration requirements [289].

Infrared (IR) spectroscopy serves as a vibrational spectroscopy method employed for evaluating the chemical composition of a given sample. IR spectrophotometer releases infrared light that is absorbed by all molecules with a non-zero dipole moment. Albert A. Michelson was the first scientist who utilized the Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) in the late 1880s [290]. The IR-active functional groups can be identified by the FT-IR technique for organic and inorganic samples within the range of 400–4000 cm−1 [291]. In this context, The IR spectra were used to assess the presence of functional groups in graphene and CNT samples [292], particularly oxygen-containing functional groups. For instance, the bond C = O stretching vibration in the carboxylic acid or carboxylate showed strong absorption at 1719 cm−1 and 1620 cm−1, respectively [293]. Blake and colleagues (2004) illustrated the existence of a covalent bond between functional molecules and CNTs. They achieved this by grafting silicon and tin-based molecules onto the nanotubes and identifying C-Si and C-Sn vibration modes in the FT-IR spectrum [294]. Baudot et al. (2010) demonstrated a method for the identification of functional groups grafted on CNTs determination and confirmed that the grafted functional groups were covalently bonded to the CNTs without any modification to their functional groups [295]. FT-IR analysis is not typically used for characterizing carbon nanomaterials due to weak intensity signals, despite being fast and inexpensive with minimal preparation requirements. Several alternative techniques such as Raman scattering and X-ray photoelectron methods may be used for this purpose instead [296].

When a sample is exposed to monochromatic electromagnetic radiation with energy ℎv0 , it can undergo reflection, absorption, or scattering in various directions. A laser beam releases photons and induces alternating polarization in the molecules, which under ambient conditions, are in their ground-vibrational state [297]. Most of the incident photons are scattered from the sample via elastic scattering, a process known as Rayleigh scattering. The wavelength of the scattered photons remains identical to that of the incident photons. The fraction of scattered radiation that is inelastic (approximately 1 in 10 million of the scattered light) has a frequency (ℎv0 +ℎv) or (ℎv0-ℎv), which differs from the frequency of the incident radiation. Here, hν represents the energy difference between vibrational states. The term Stokes Raman scattering is used for the parts with lower frequency (ℎv0-ℎv), while the scattered radiation with higher frequency is mentioned as anti-Stokes scattering. The alteration in wavelength of the scattered photons, caused by variations in the polarizability of the molecules, gives structural details about the sample [298,299].

Raman spectroscopy is an exceptional, noninvasive research tool for graphene identification, graphene layer number determination, stress and defects assessment, and exploration of various crucial mechanical, electrical, and optical parameters [300]. The single-layer graphene spectrum has two prominent features. The first is the G band, which appears at 1580 cm−1 and is assigned to the C–C stretching. The G band is usually present in most graphite-based materials, including diamond, carbon nanotubes, highly ordered pyrolytic graphite, and amorphous carbon. The second band is the D band at 1350 cm−1 and refers to the disorder or defect. The 2D band at about 2680 cm−1 is initiated from the second-order Raman scattering process. Another low-intensity band at 2440 cm−1 called the G* band is also observed. However, the G* band has not been used because of its intensity [301,302].

The positions and intensities of G and 2D bands are usually used for the number of graphene layer determinations. The position of the G band is significantly affected by the number of graphene layers and provides a hint about the thickness of the graphene layer. The position of the G band shifts downward as the thickness of the graphene layer increases. Also, the intensity of the G band is positively proportional to the layer number. In the same manner, the position, shape, and intensity of the 2D band are contingent on the number of graphene layers. When graphene has one layer, the 2D band appears singular. However, with the increasing number of graphene layers, it divides into several overlapping modes due to symmetry reduction. The intensity ratio of the G to 2D band provides information about the number of graphene layers, as the peak intensity of both bands exhibits information about graphene sample quality. Accordingly, the IG/I2D for the defect-free single graphene layer is about 0.7 [303–305]. The ratio of the D band intensity to the G band intensity (ID/IG) can be utilized to evaluate the disorder levels in graphene. Two distinct stages delineate the introduction of defects: the initial stage depicts the shift from pristine graphite to nanocrystalline graphite (characterized by low defect density), while the subsequent stage involves the transition from nanocrystalline graphite to predominantly sp2 amorphous carbon (characterized by high defect density) [306]. Tuinistra- Koenig equations describe the defects from the first class [307]:

Where C(λ) approximately equals 2.4*10-10λ4 and La is the average distance between defects [308].

During the second stage, a growing quantity of defects leads to the formation of carbon clusters. These clusters gradually diminish in size, with fewer and more distorted rings, eventually progressing to the point where they start to unfold. In this context, the appearance of G and D peaks is due to the relative motion of the sp2 carbon atom and breathing modes of rings, respectively [309]. In this case, the intensity of G can be assumed to be constant as a function of disorder, and the loss of sp2 rings will lower the D band intensity, resulting in the Tuinstra–Koenig equation not being valid, and the following equation should be used instead [306].

Raman spectroscopy is also used in carbon nanotube characterization. Raman spectra for the SWNCTs and MWCNTs contain a G band at 1590 cm−1 and a D band at 1320 cm−1. The intensities ratio (ID/IG) refers to the quality of the CNTs and is proportionally related to the number of defects [310]. The SWNCT and MWCNT spectra have several differences. Besides G and D peaks, the radial breathing modes (RBM) are recorded at the SWNCT’s spectrum below 300 cm−1. RBM demonstrates information about the diameters and interaction of the nanotubes. The absence of these bands in MWCNT’s spectra is typically attributed to the weak signal emitted by the outer tube, which possesses a larger diameter, making it difficult to detect. Also, the signal is broadened due to the influence of inner tube diameters. The second difference between the spectra is that there are two separate G bands in the SWNCT’s spectrum (G + and G-) and more conspicuous D bands [311]. While in the MWCNT’s spectrum, 2G and 2D bands are noticed around 3000 cm−1 [312,313]

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is one of the analytical methods for surface analysis that is capable of discerning and quantifying the chemical and electronic states, elemental composition, and empirical formula of the elements contained within 10 nm. XPS provides detection sensitivity ranging from 0.1 % to 1 % for elements excluding hydrogen and helium [314]. In the XPS, the sample surface is exposed to monoenergetic X-ray radiation, which is more than enough to release electrons from its surface. The ejected electrons from distinct energy levels are received and detected by a hemispherical analyzer, which measures their kinetic energy (KE) with the aid of binding energy Eb and electron work function (ϕ: the energy for leaving the material surface) [315].

In carbon-based nanomaterials, XPS is utilized in the functional group characterization, particularly the functional groups that contain oxygen in graphene and CNTs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) can differentiate between various bond groups and ascertain the oxidation states of carbon atoms (C–C, C-O, and C = O), providing valuable insights into molecular structures and chemical environments [124,316]. Also, the XPS analysis for carbon atoms has been employed for GO reduction into rGO [317].

The characterization of nanomaterials encompasses considerations such as size, shape, polymorphism, and crystal structure. X-ray diffraction (XRD) is a primary technique for evaluating nanomaterials. It provides insights into the polymorphs present, crystallographic structure, crystallinity, and crystallite size. During XRD testing, the wavelength of the X-rays used closely matches the interatomic distances within the material. When X-rays interact with a crystalline sample, they generate a diffraction pattern characterized by distinct, sharp spots known as Bragg diffraction peaks. The obtained diffractogram from the analyzed nanomaterial consists of a set of Bragg diffraction peaks, which can be analyzed in terms of location and intensity to determine the material’s crystallographic structure [318].

XRD has been employed extensively in graphite oxidation process monitoring and the consequential exfoliation of graphite oxide to GO. In XRD graphs, pristine graphite shows a basal reflection (002) peak at 2θ = 26° with an interlayer distance of 0.34 nm. The graphite oxide presents it at 2θ = 12° with 0.7 nm interlayer distance. As a result of oxygen species intercalation between the graphite layers, an expansion in the graphite oxide interlayer is noticed. As oxidation progresses, the intensity of the (002) diffraction line gradually diminishes and eventually vanishes. Simultaneously, the intensity of the diffraction peak at 12° increases with oxidation. Once graphite oxide is fully exfoliated, the peak at 12° also disappears [319–321].

Regarding CNTs, single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) typically aggregate into crystalline bundles, exhibiting a peak at around 2θ = 6° in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern. This technique enables the calculation of the number of SWCNTs within each bundle. Multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), on the other hand, display a reflection peak at 2θ = 26°, akin to graphite's (002) peak. This signal has been utilized to monitor the growth of MWCNTs [322,323].

While X-ray diffraction (XRD) is a widely utilized technique for determining material structure at the atomic level, its applications are constrained by several factors. Challenges in growing crystals and the capability to obtain results only from a single conformation or binding state of the sample limit the versatility of the XRD technique. Additionally, XRD suffers from the drawback of relatively low intensity of diffracted X-rays, especially noticeable for materials with low atomic numbers. These limitations have been highlighted in several studies [324–326].

Electron microscopy differs from optical microscopy in that the EM employs accelerated electron beams and electrostatic lenses to obtain higher resolutions by leveraging the much shorter wavelengths of electrons compared to photons of visible light [296]. SEM, or scanning electron microscopy, is a surface imaging technique wherein an incident electron beam scans across the sample surface [327]. During this scanning process, the electron beam interacts with the sample, generating various signals that reflect both the atomic composition and topographic details of the specimen surface [328–330]. Several emissions occur when incident electrons interact with the sample surface in SEM. These include elastic scattering of electrons, known as backscattered electrons, as well as the inelastic scattering of electrons, referred to as low-energy secondary electrons. Additionally, characteristic X-ray light, called cathodoluminescence, is emitted from the atoms found on the sample surface or in near-surface material [329]. In SEM, secondary electron detection is the most common mode, as it allows for achieving resolutions smaller than 1 nm [329].

The primary drawback of SEM is its necessity to operate in high vacuum conditions, which restricts its application to solid samples in many commercial instruments. Furthermore, SEM primarily provides information on conductive materials, limiting its versatility. However, this limitation can be mitigated by techniques, such as sputtering a thin layer of a conductive material, like silver or gold, onto the sample surface. Nevertheless, this approach may modify nanomaterials by adding layers, potentially impacting particle size and morphology. However, SEM has been extensively used in NM characterization for sample morphology and topology [331].

SEM technology has recently been coupled with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) to determine the microscale elements on the sample surface. Also, SEM-EDX has been employed in acquiring data related to size, shape, and surface texture in a short time [332].

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) is capable of revealing details about the grain boundaries, microstructure of interfaces, and crystalline defects present in nanoparticles with a level of atomic dimensions (<1 nm) [333]. An incident electron beam is passed through a delicate foil specimen, to achieve such resolution. During this process, the incident electrons undergo interactions with the specimen, resulting in their transformation into unscattered, elastically scattered, or inelastically scattered electrons. A series of electromagnetic lenses are utilized to focus these electrons. The focused electrons are directed onto a screen. This process generates electron diffraction patterns, amplitude-contrast images, phase-contrast images, or shadow images, with varying degrees of darkness determined by the density of unscattered electrons [334]. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) provides superior resolution, enabling a more precise examination of morphological details (inner structure) and topographical features. Moreover, electron microscopy is often paired with energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) to determine sample composition through elemental analysis [335].

HRTEM has been employed to differentiate between SWCNT and MWCNT by measuring their diameters. Also, HRTEM-EDX can detect the carbonaceous and catalytic NPs [336]. Besides, HRTEM-EDX has been used in SWCNT’s identification through chiral indices determination, fullerenes identification, and conformational changes visualization [337–339].

Single-layer graphene can be visualized as semitransparent sheets in the TEM technique [122]. The number of graphene layers can be determined by observing foldings at the rim of the membrane, especially where the sheet is locally parallel to the electron beam [340]. Additionally, distinguishing between single-layer and two-layer graphene is possible through their respective nano-area electron diffraction patterns, which can be obtained by altering the incidence angles between the electron beam and the graphene sheet [340–342]. Furthermore, electron diffraction patterns also serve to monitor graphene roughness [340].

TEM and SEM are valuable techniques for revealing the size and shape of nanomaterials (NMs). However, TEM holds an advantage over SEM due to its enhanced spatial resolution and the ability to perform supplementary analytical measurements, particularly when coupled with techniques like electron energy loss spectroscopy and EDX. These analytical methods offer quantitative insights into the electronic structure and chemical composition of NMs [328,334].

The advantages of TEM come with certain limitations. Operating in a high vacuum and requiring a narrow sample section is essential for electron-beam penetration. Consequently, high-resolution imaging only examines a tiny fraction of the specimen, leading to poor statistical sampling. Additionally, TEM provides 2D imaging, lacking depth sensitivity for 3D specimens. Moreover, samples must be narrow enough to transmit adequate electrons for image generation, often necessitating extensive conditioning of specimens. This conditioning process raises the risk of altering the sample structure, which increases the time required for TEM analysis [331].

Atomic force microscope (AFM) is a type of scanning probe microscope (SPM). They possess the capability to image almost any surface, including biological samples, glass, polymers, composites, and ceramics. Additionally, AFM can be utilized to measure and localize various forces, such as mechanical properties, magnetic forces, and adhesion strength. AFMs operates in two primary modes: tapping mode and contact mode. In tapping mode, the AFM cantilever vibrates above the sample surface, allowing the tip to intermittently contact the surface. Contrastingly, in contact mode, the AFM tip remains continuously in contact with the surface throughout the imaging process [332].

AFMs have higher flexibility in working environments and sample preparation requirements compared to electron microscopes. AFM can detect the shape, size, and mechanical properties of conductors, semiconductors, insulators, and biological samples in many environments such as atmosphere, high vacuum, and liquid. Moreover, the resolution in the AFMs is very high (<0.1 nm for horizontal and < 0.01 nm for vertical). AFM enables the measurement of various physical and mechanical properties of materials at the nanometer scale, including electrical conductivity, stagnation, friction, and lubrication [332].

AFMs can be used in monolayer graphene identifications [343]. The van der Waals thickness for the pristine monolayer graphene is about 0.34 nm [120]. However, AFMs cannot be used in single-layer graphene thickness measurements. The recorded values significantly vary over a wide range (0.4––1.7 nm). This inaccuracy can be explained by the interaction between the tip and the surface, the settings for image feedback, the chemistry of the sample surface, and the presence of a layer of absorbed water [344,345].

AFM provides insights into the presence of macromolecules on graphene surfaces and offers details about the graphene sheets' mechanical, frictional, electrical, magnetic, and elastic properties. Regarding carbon nanotubes (CNTs), AFM enables the differentiation between single-walled (SWCNTs) and double-walled (DWCNTs) variants. AFM has successfully detected metallic particles and biomolecules attached to CNTs and graphene surfaces [346].

CBNs are explored for their biomedical applications due to their excellent physicochemical properties. This section highlights recent advances in the biomedical applications of CBNs, specifically in the field of drug delivery, bioimaging and biosensing.

For the efficient therapeutic effect of the drug, it requires a quality drug delivery system. An ideal drug carrier should show high biocompatibility, high water solubility, low cytotoxicity and long-term biostability [347–349]. As CBNs have a very small size they can effectively bind with the biomolecules and because of the abundance of functional groups they can easily be modified to serve the purpose [350–352]. The majority of CBNs have very high zeta potential which prevents aggregation and provides high stability to the particles [353–355].

Since pH significantly influences the interaction between drugs and carriers, it ultimately determines the effectiveness of the drug delivery system [350,356]. In other words, if a drug can be released based on pH it can result in the sustained supply of drug to the target. Therefore, it is important to develop a drug delivery system that shows targeted therapeutic effect in line with the pH of the target. A wide range of recent studies is focused on finding a suitable drug carrier for cancer. In contrast to normal cells, cancerous cells have acidic pH, which underscores the role of pH in controlled drug delivery for cancer therapy. CBNs can be strong candidates in this regard [352]. In fact, there is a handsome number of studies that show that CBNs have changes in their interaction with drugs based on the pH of the environment which can be very useful in this case. For example, Sangjan et al. used a one-pot hydrothermal technique to prepare polyethylene glycol functionalized CDs (CD-PEG) from oil palm empty fruit bunch [357]. The synthesized materials were used as a drug delivery system for doxorubicin (DOX) to colon cancer cells. They evaluated drug release under acidic and neutral conditions where a pH of 5.0 represented an acidic medium while that of 7.4 represented a neutral medium. At the pH of 5.0, they noticed that the drug was released at a fast pace for the first 30 h. Then it attained equilibrium with 23 %, being the highest amount of drug release. Meanwhile, the drug release rate was slower for the pH of 7.4 with only 36 % of the drug could be released after 4 days. The possible explanation for this behavior might be that at low pH the π–π interactions in the hydrophobic bond between CDs-PEG and DOX were weakened [358], and electrostatic interactions between PEG and Dox were also hampered which caused the release of DOX [359,360]. But overall release was slow owing to the strong hydrophobic interactions between CDs and the drug. A recent study carried out by Xie et al. on the delivery of doxorubicin using CDs as the carrier showed a similar trend but with an improved release rate[351]. They synthesized CDs through aldol condensation. As prepared CDs were then reacted with DOX using Schiff base reaction. Eventually, the CDs-DOX@cubosome anticancer nanosystem was obtained by loading the pH-responsive CDs-DOX system into cubosomes. The nanocarrier was checked for its in vitro release of DOX by dialysis method. It was performed in buffer solutions with different pHs. The different pHs represented different physiological environments. Overall drug release from CDs-DOX after 72 h was found to be 35.1 %, 46.8 %, and 81.2 % at pH 7.4, 6.8, and 5.0 respectively. It is clear from the results that the release rate for DOX was relatively higher under acidic conditions. Moreover, the release rate in an acidic medium is almost triple as compared to that in a neutral medium. It is opined that low pH damages the imine bond which leads to faster release behavior [361,362]. It can be said that CBNs as a drug delivery system, in general, show the highest drug release in acidic medium and very minimal release in basic or neutral media. However, there are still some cases where results did not follow the mentioned pattern. It can be seen in a study conducted by Wibrianto et al. that boron-doped carbon dots carrying naproxen had higher drug release at neutral pH than at acidic pH [349]. It was proposed that slow diffusion of medium solution entering BCD resulted in a low dissolution percentage of the drug in acidic and basic media.
