Another study on naproxen having BCDs as a delivery system, conducted by Wibrianto et al. displayed similar results [356]. The authors suggested that both acidic and alkaline media promoted hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interaction between the drug and the carrier resulting in low release rates. Like drug release, drug and carrier interactions have a key role when it comes to drug loading and entrapment. For instance, polyethylene glycol (PEG)/GO)/silk fibroin (SF) hydrogel-nanocomposite and doxorubicin formulations had high entrapment (%EE) and loading efficacy (%LE) 87.75 ± 0.7 % and 46 ± 1 %, respectively [354]. The large specific surface of GO [363], π-π stacking as well as the hydrogen bond between GO sheets and DOX [364], and the electrostatic interaction between the drug and SF were considered the prime reason for it [365]. Similarly, Fahmi et al. examined in vitro and in vivo application of 2-aminophenyl boronic acid surface modified carbon dots (APBA-CDs) for delivery of chalcone where drug-CBNs interacted through hydrogen bonding, electrostatic and π-π stacking [350]. It was found to have 99.99 % drug loading efficiency with HeLa cell’s viability over 91 % in a concentration range of concentration 5–400 μg/mL).

The mechanism of release is also important in this regard. As a matter of fact, one drug delivery system can have more than one release mechanism. Consequently, different papers show different outcomes. As an example, Wibrianto et al. reported independence of the release mechanism from pH [356]. They inspected the drug release for naproxen by changing pH to 4, 7 and 9. They used the dialysis method to examine the effect of pH on the release profile of the drug and eventually its effect on the release mechanism. For this purpose, they took 5 mL of 200 ppm of B-CDs/Naproxen and sealed it in a dialysis bag immersed in PBS. The dialysis was performed for 24 h at 300 rpm and at room temperature. 1 mL of the sample solution was taken out from the dialysis system at specified time intervals. The total amount of the external phase was conserved by replacing the removed amount of sample with an equal amount of PBS. Results for Chi-square (χ2) and R-square (R2) calculations showed that the drug release from B-CD/Naproxen followed the Peppas-Sahlin model for all three pH conditions. Based on the Peppas–Sahlin model it was concluded that the drug release displayed the Fickian diffusion. Apart from that, there was a change in the state of the polymer from a semirigid state to a flexible state which led to relaxation in the polymer chain [366]. The relative values of K1 and K2 determine the drug release mechanism in this model. In the case where K1 is greater than K2, the drug release mechanism is dominated by Fickian diffusion [367]. In the case where K1 is less than K2 polymer chain relaxation dominates influencing the mechanism. As K1 had a greater magnitude than K2 in the experiment, it was concluded that the diffusion mechanism was favored over polymer chain relaxation for drug release. On the contrary, Kesavan et al. reported dependence of the release mechanism on the pH [355]. They prepared a pH-responsive drug delivery system by modifying GO with Chitosan (CS) and Xyloglucan (XG). Kappa carrageenan (κ-C) was used as a model drug to examine in vitro usefulness of the as-prepared drug carrier targeted for cancer. The nanohybrid loaded with drug i.e. GO-CS-XG-κ-C was incubated in PBS at pH 5.3 and 7.4. The total drug release percentage from the nanohybrid was evaluated for fitness with different kinetic drug release models. The best-fit release model was determined by the values of the correlation coefficient. The results showed that the values of the correlation coefficient changed with pH. Specifically, at pH 7.4 correlation coefficient for zero order had the highest value indicating the fitness of data with zero order kinetics at that pH. In other words, a pH of 7.4, concentration did not affect the diffusion rate of the drug. At pH 5.3, first-order, Higuchi and zero-order had correlation coefficients of 0.9750, 0.9629 and 0.9781 respectively. It indicated that drug release behavior was of a mixed nature which had a dependence on concentration, diffusion or swelling mechanism and diffusion by surface area. The in vitro release data was also verified for its fitness by using Korsmeyer Peppas equation for both pH 7.4 and 5.3. The correlation coefficient for pH 7.4 and 5.3 was found to be in the range of 0.82–––0.98 which is a high value that is indicator of a good correlation. The fitting curve was used to calculate the constant (K) and the exponents (n). Besides, the drug release was also explained by using the value of Korsmeyer-Peppas model. It was reported that at pH 7.4, n was 0.75 and K was 1.25 indicating the mechanism of the drug release was non-Fickian i.e. the release showed anomalous diffusion. Meanwhile, n was 0.427 and K was found to be 1.78, it suggested that the Fickian diffusion was observed for that pH value. Jeshvaghani et al. reported the fabrication polyethylene glycol (PEG)/ GO/silk fibroin (SF) hydrogel-nanocomposite using double nano-emulsification method [354]. They focused on the delivery of DOX towards the enhancement of cancer treatment. The dialysis method was employed to inspect the release mechanism of the drug from the PEG/GO/SF nanocomposites. The dialysis was performed at pH 7.4 and 5.4. The pH of 7.4 represented neutral healthy tissue environment whereas pH 5.4 was presentative of the acidic tumor tissue environment. As usual, the data obtained from the experiment was tested for its fitness with different kinetic models of drug release. The results showed that at pH 5.4 zero-order model didn’t have a high value of correlation coefficient which meant that the release at that pH didn’t show independence from the concentration. In other words, the drug release could be dependent on concentration. At the same pH, the first-order model had good conformity which further confirmed that the release was affected by the concentration. That is to say, when there is a larger amount of drug in the carrier there is a higher release of the drug. In contrast to this, it is lower when there is a smaller amount of drug left in the carrier. Therefore, the release profile at the beginning and the end showed great difference. On the other hand, the difference in the release rate between the beginning and ending for pH 7.4 was found to be smaller. It was also observed that the release of the drug obeyed the Korsmeyer-Peppas model for both neutral and acidic pHs. Also, the value of release exponent (n) was found to be 0.801 at pH 7.4 and 0.484 at pH 5.4 i.e. it was greater than 0.43. When the value of n is greater than 0.43 it indicates anomalous release behavior. Therefore, it was concluded that the drug had a non-Fickian anomalous release mechanism. This kind of mechanism involves factors like diffusion, swelling and relaxation of the carrier system and dissolution [368]. As n decreased with a decrease in pH which is an indication that the drug release at low pH was mainly controlled by diffusion [369]. Whereas at the neutral pH, the release was primarily affected by carrier dissolution. Furthermore, the value of K increased as the pH value decreased. Therefore, it can be assumed that the release was faster at low pH compared to that at high pH [370]. Application of the Hixson-Crowell model, at neutral pH, revealed its higher level of conformity with the data compared to other models. It was in line with the fact that at neutral pH the release was controlled by dissolution and the model is used for the drug delivery systems that involve dissolution mechanism [371]. Meanwhile Baker model showed higher conformity in acidic environment. This result was supported by the fact that the model is used for spherical matrix with diffusion mechanism [372], and the drug was found to have diffusion mechanism of release in acidic medium. Besides, Higuchi model also showed reasonable conformity which further confirmed the results from Korsmeyer-Peppas model [369].

As seen in Table 1a, 1b, cell viability for CBNs was found to be ∼ 80 % in contrast to the CBNs loaded with drugs which showed their biocompatibility. In general, CBNs are not toxic but as a carrier, they can enhance the cytotoxicity of the drug by increasing its cellular uptake. It is evident from the data in research carried out by Sangjan et al that fibroblast cells (normal cells) had high cell viability of about 85 % when incubated with CDs-PEG at 3.9 μg/mL, concentration beyond that caused toxicity to fibroblast cells [357]. On the contrary, CaCo-2 cells (cancer cells) experienced high toxicity from CDs-PEG at a concentration of 0.12 μg/mL. Results showed that cell viability of CaCo-2 cells was less than 85 % for CDs-PEG loaded with DOX having a concentration of 0.011 μg/mL, whereas cell viability of fibroblast remained over 85 % for DOX loaded CDs-PEG at 370 μg/mL. It was found to be in line with the fact that CaCo-2 cells had higher cellular uptake of the formulations than that for normal fibroblast cells. As already mentioned, uptake is necessary for the drug efficacy. The low solubility of the potential drugs and difficulty in penetrating barriers in the living system can pose problems in this regard. For example, a promising drug-like genistein also suffers from these drawbacks. Nevertheless, a good carrier can solve this problem. In their work, Mehta and Shende proposed a solution to this very problem by loading the drug on carbon-based materials [373]. They inspected fullerene (C60) and multi-wall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) as a potential carrier for genistein (GEN)and its subsequent efficacy in treating amyloid-β (Aβ) plaques in Alzheimer’s disease. Ex-vivo experiments showed that the drug had sustained release from both OPC60 and OP-MWCNTs. After 24 hm release percentage for OPC60 and OP-MWCNTs was observed to be 72.16 ± 1.15 % and 77.12 ± 2.15 %, respectively. In vitro analysis of drug release also showed that the nano-formulations had controlled drug release. The hydrophobic nature of GEN, OPC60, and OP-MWCNTs provided longer residence time in the donor compartment which caused steady and controlled release of the drug. These experiments further revealed that the small size and hydrophobic nature of the nanomaterials facilitated the penetration of the drug through goat nasal mucosa. This very nature of the nanomaterials improved the diffusion of the drug through the tight junctions of the nasal epithelial cells. Improved diffusion of the drug led to better uptake by nasal receptors namely scavenger receptors class B type 1 (SR-B1). These receptors have a vital role in the receptor-based transport through the epithelial surface. Therefore, enhancement of permeation of GEN across the nasal mucosa by the application of OPC60 and OP-MWCNTs helps the targeted action of the drug within the nasal epithelium. It indicated that this combination of carbon-based nanomaterials and GEN can be used for the nasal administration of the drug. In-vivo experiments were also carried out to further evaluate the efficacy of the drug in the brain. Albino Wistar rats were used for the experiment. The concentration of the aqueous drug suspension in the brains of the rats was monitored with time. The data unveiled that, after 24 h, low amounts of 3.25 ± 0.52 % of the drug were found in rat brains after nasal administration of aqueous drug suspension. It is evident that aqueous suspension of the drug was not able to cross the blood–brain barrier (BBB) and nasal mucosa. As already mentioned, this problem is caused by the low solubility of the drug. On the other hand, after loading the drug on OPC60 and MWCNTs an improvement was observed in the permeation of the drug. Based on the literature findings authors proposed the following mechanism for this improvement in the permeation. OPC60 has a small size (<500 nm) as well as a high surface charge (>25 mV). These properties of OPC60 enabled them to permeate the nasal epithelium through multiple pathways. Small-sized OPC60 can cross tight junctions of epithelial cells by passive diffusion. This way they can enter the olfactory lobe and from there they can have access to the brain [374]. Another possible mechanism for the permeation of the OPC60 is endocytosis via mechanisms such as clathrin-mediated endocytosis, and caveolae-mediated endocytosis [375]. This cellular process starts with the engulfing of the nanoparticles by the cell into vesicles. The subsequent step is the transport of the engulfed nanoparticle to the cytoplasm. Transferrin receptors SR-B1 facilitate the transport of the fullerene nanomaterials across the nasal epithelium. The initial step for this transcytosis across the membrane is the binding of fullerene to the receptor. MWCNTs also exhibited identical permeation. In other words, MWCNTS was first taken up by the nasal mucosa through passive diffusion and then clathrin-mediated endocytosis, caveolae-mediated endocytosis was responsible for the transcytosis across the membrane. But unlike fullerene, it was not the sole receptor involved in transcytosis. In addition, SR-B1, the Toll-Like Receptors (TLRs) also participated in the in receptor-mediated transcytosis of MWCNTs across the nasal epithelium [376]. Despite improvement in permeation of the drug through nasal mucosa carbon nanotubes might still pose some problems. As pointed out by Xiaowei et al. that it has limited drug loading, and it has low water solubility [377]. Furthermore, long carbon nanotubes (CNTs) can be toxic. Therefore, to make up for these demerits of the carbon nanotubes they suggested functionalization of the nanotubes with ethosomes. They opined that carbon chain of the CNTs can be shortened and they can have an increase in water solubility after functionalization. This study further highlighted the utilization of these functionalized CNTs for transdermal delivery of ketoprofen (KP). For this purpose, they loaded KP functionalized single-walled carbon nanotube (f-SWCNTs) composite ethosomes (f-SWCNTs-KP-ES) and analyzed their penetration. As expected, there was an improvement in the penetration of the of the f-SWCNTs-KP-ES gel. A comparative study of permeation of f-SWCNTs-KP-ES gel, commercial gel, drug-ethosomes gel (KP-ES gel), f-SWCNTs-KP gel and KP API gel was also conducted. The results showed that f-SWCNTs-KP-ES gel had the highest permeation of all the gels that were investigated. It was further stated that it is not clear how carbon nanotubes improve permeation through skin. In fact, it is found that carbon nanotubes are not able to cross the skin and have access to the body. However, literature only suggests internalization of the CNTs by living cells and their penetration through biofilms. Moreover, the authors highlighted that storage effect, that is to say adsorption and desorption of the drug has been reported to be involved in improved penetration of the drug [378,379]. Results further indicated that loading on CNTs raised the solubility of the drug. On the downside, drug release became sluggish and absorption of the drug through the skin was limited. In fact, the combination of the drug with ethosomes had superior permeation when compared to that of the functionalized carbon nanotubes with the drug. As an account for these results, it was suggested that the presence of ethanol in ethosomes might have played a role in the improvement of permeation of the KP-ES [380]. Ethanol is known to breakdown multi-layered structures composed of lipid. It can also lower the structural density and enforce the fluidity of the outermost layer of the skin [381,382]. They further highlighted that the changes in the transition temperature due to the fusion of the phospholipids were also a contributing factor in the improvement of the penetration [383].

The effects of CBNs on Drug Penetration and Cytotoxicity are illustrated in Fig. 4 . An effective drug delivery system ensures maximum drug efficacy. In other words, the drug should produce the desired effect. For example, in the case of cancer drug should be able to induce death in cancer cells or it should inhibit cell growth. Studies involving CBNs also dedicate one section of their investigation for the determination of the cell death or inhibition of cell proliferation caused by drug and carrier combination. In their study related to the application of CBNs for drug delivery, Vasanthakumar et al. loaded Cisplatin (CDDP) on chitosan-functionalized GO nanocomposite (CDDP@CS-GO NC) and investigated the drug efficacy [384]. Annexin V-fluorescein 5-isothiocyanate (FITC) assay was employed to assess the cell death caused by the drug and nanocarrier formulation CDDP@CS-GO NC. HeLa cells were used as model cells for this study. In brief, HeLa cells were incubated with different concentrations of CDDP@CS-GO NCs. After 24 h incubation, the formulation was washed with PBS and analyzed to determine the ratio of cells experiencing programmed cell death. This programmed cell death increases cell death in cancer cells. Comparative analysis of cell death caused by free CDDP, and the CDDP-loaded CS-GO NCs was also performed. Results show that early apoptosis induced by free CDDP and CDDP-loaded CS-GO NCs was 11.58 % and 11.93 %, respectively. Meanwhile, late apoptosis caused by free CDDP and CS-GO NCs loaded with CDDP was 12.21 % AND 21.79 %. It can be said that the drug loaded on the CBNs carriers had an overall improvement in its ability to induce apoptosis. This rise in cell apoptosis was attributed to the increased cellular uptake of the drug-loaded on the carrier. To further confirm apoptosis, they performed protein expression analysis. Western blotting was employed to inspect Bcl-2 and caspase-3 protein expression in the cells treated with free CDDP and CDDP@CS-GO NC. Data revealed that cells treated with CDDP@CS-GO NC experienced a significant dip in the expression of Bcl-2 protein expression as compared to those treated with free CDDP. On the other hand, there was a rise in the expression of caspase-3 protein in the cells treated with drugs loaded on the CS-GO NC nano delivery system. The results support the occurrence of apoptosis because Bcl-2 is a protein that hinders apoptosis and caspase-3 is a protein that promotes apoptosis. Bcl-2 hampers programmed cell death by binding to the mitochondrial membrane which causes a decline in the release of cytochrome-c. On the other hand, caspase-3 causes the breakdown of the Poly (ADP-ribose) polymerase (PARP) which induces cell death. Similarly, Jahedi and Meshkini reported the preparation of GO-based nanoparticles as a delivery system for 5-fluorouracil [385]. Precisely, Ceria nanoparticles (CeNPs) were loaded with 5-FU and were coated with calcium carbonate nanoparticles (CaNPs). This combination of drug and nanoparticles was then functionalized with folinic acid (FA) to obtain Ca.FU.Ce.FA nanocomposites (NCs). The nanocomposites were connected with GO and adorned with silver nanoparticles (Ag°NPs) which resulted in the preparation of drug delivery nano-systems (FU.FA@NS). The formulation was then loaded on mesenchymal stem cell (MSCs) membranes. Thus, they obtained FU.FA@NS.MSCs which were expected to have targeted therapeutic effect on tumor with minimum side-effects. The as prepared formulation was tested for its anti-tumor efficacy. The analysis of antitumor potency of FU.FA@NS.MSCs under UVA was performed. For this purpose, transwall co-culture system was used to co-culture multiple doses of MSCs with CT26. To evaluate the cell viability of the CT26 cells in the presence of FU.FA@NS.MSCs, first the formulation was photoactivated. After incubation of 48 h MTT analysis was performed. Results of MTT analysis showed that viable percentage of CT26 cells had decline with increase in the dose of FU.FA@NS.MSCs. Only 40 % of cancer cells could survive at the highest amount of the nanoengineered MSCs (5 × 104 cells). Moreover, post-irradiation in vitro cytotoxicity of FU.FA@NS.MSCs was also examined using phase-contrast microscopic examination of CT26 cells. It was noticed that FU.FA@NS loaded on MSCs, at IC50 value of 3.09 × 104 cells having FU.FA@NSs at a concentration of 0.154 µg mL−1, had better cytotoxicity against tumour compared to bare FU.FA@NS. It was reckoned that superior antitumor effect by FU.FA@NS-loaded MSCs was achieved because of greater permeation. It also indicated that modified MSCs had sufficient drug loading to cause such an effect. Furthermore, unmodified MSCs were also evaluated for their inhibitory effects on tumor cells. When treated with different densities of the unmodified MSCs, cancer cells still maintained a survival percentage of 90 %. It can be said that bare MSCs have negligible inhibitory effects on cancer cell growth. Qualitative and quantitative tests were carried out to further understand the mechanism of the inhibitory effect induced by FU.FA@NS.MSCs. To serve the purpose, CT26 cells and FU.FA@NS.MSCs co-cultured for 4 and 24 h. ICP-OES results revealed supernatant of the cancer had an increase in the amount of Ag and Ce elements which reflected that FU.FA@NS.MSCs exhibited tumor-specific release of the formulation. Besides, the appearance of green fluorescence in the cytoplasm of FU.FA@NS.MSCs-treated CT26 cells after 4 h of treatment was an indication of the uptake of nanoengineered MSCs by CT26 cells. Meanwhile, 24 h of co-incubation of the cancer cells with FU.FA@NS.MSCs resulted in a hike in the fluorescence intensity. It means that the MSCs displayed a time-dependent release of the drug composite.

Gene therapy has seen remarkable strides, giving rise to a new class of treatments that can modify the course of diseases. However, one of the main obstacles is ensuring the efficient delivery of adequate genetic material into the appropriate cells, which is essential for successful gene transfer. Over the past ten years, various viral and non-viral vectors have been explored to address this challenge, with carbon nanotubes (CNTs) emerging as a promising option for non-viral delivery [386].

In a previous study, a nano-graphene oxide (NGO) platform (30–50 nm) that integrates cyanine5 (Cy5)-modified miRNA (let-7i) (Cy5-miRNA) and platinum (Pt) for the dual purpose of detecting and reversing drug resistance, while also treating cisplatin-resistant ovarian cancer cells (SKOV3DDP). The Cy5-miRNA, adsorbed onto the NGO, selectively binds to drug resistance-related mRNA, suppressing its expression and triggering the release of Cy5-miRNA from the NGO. This release restores the Cy5 fluorescence, enabling real-time monitoring of drug resistance[387].

Wu et al. (2019) utilize a nano-graphene oxide with antisense walR RNA to inhibit the pathogenicity of Enterococcus faecalis in periapical periodontitis. The GO effectively delivered the ASwalR plasmid into E.faecalis cells, leading to a significant reduction in the expression of virulence-associated genes. Additionally, GO-PEI-ASwalR inhibited biofilm formation and enhanced bactericidal activity in infected canal models in vitro. In a four-week periapical infection rat model, treatment with GO-PEI-ASwalR notably reduced the size of periapical lesions [388].

Carbon-based nanostructures have become increasingly viable candidates for the delivery of DNA, RNA, vaccines, and drugs in the past decade, thanks to their unique properties and versatility. Recent research highlights the potential of graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes, and related materials in advancing gene therapy, mRNA vaccine development, and drug delivery systems. While there is substantial progress in their application, challenges related to toxicity, scalability, and immune response need to be addressed before these materials can be widely adopted in clinical settings. The next decade is expected to see continued refinement of CBNs-based nanocarriers, with an increased focus on personalized medicine and targeted therapies.

CBNs, with high quantum yield and excellent stability in a wide range of pH, hold great potential in the field of diagnosis and bioimaging [389,390]. A plethora of research is focused on exploring bioimaging applications of carbon dots [350,391,392]. CDs are zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials that have a carbon core and an amorphous shell with groups containing oxygen [389,392–394]. Studies reveal that they show both excitation-dependent [350,356,390] and independent emission [392,392] based on surface characteristics. Interestingly, in a study emission from PVP passivated carbon dots (PPCCDs) was found to have excitation independence at and below 400 nm whereas excitation dependence above 400 nm [395]. Passivated CDs have uniform surface emissive traps which enables them to avoid autofluorescence at lower wavelengths and cause excitation-independent emission. On the other hand, various surface emissive traps cause dependence on the excitation wavelength in CDs. In addition, passivation of the CD surface with organics or polymers gives rise to the new electronic energy states which leads to a reduction in the binding energy of the excitons and results in more efficient radiative recombination. Similarly, modifications can also lead to improvement in photoluminescence in most of the cases modified CBNs produce higher quantum yields compared to their unmodified counterparts. For instance, bare CDs had 12.4 % QY while their modified version with 2-aminophenyl boronic acid (APBA) revealed QY up to 75 %. It is clear from the data that the type of modifier also affects the overall properties of the CBNs, including their PL properties [350]. [396] compared N-doped CDs with S-doped CDs. N-CDs showed higher PL intensity and higher QY comparatively. In a recent work by Kumar et al. CQDs were prepared from 20 coded amino acids and were investigated for their bioimaging [393]. They had the highest refractive index (RI)ever reported. All the amino acid–carbon quantum dots (AA-CQDs) had variable RI but the CQDs derived from positively charged amino acids showed higher RI. The highest value was reported for arginine-CQDs (∼2.1). In fact, when compared to previous studies, arginine-CQDs also had relatively higher QY% ∼86 % and longer decay time (∼8.0 ns). AA-CQDs were also tested for their cytotoxicity. Results indicate that human breast cancer cells (HeLa) maintained a 95 % survival rate after 24 h incubation with AA-CQDs at a concentration of 100 μg/mL. All in all, CBNs had low cytotoxicity with GO showing no toxicity in one of the research projects. As seen in Table 1a, 1b cell viability is still around 80 % even after incubation with CBNs at 1000 µg/mL.

In vitro cell imaging results from different studies indicate that CBNs were internalized by endocytosis and in most cases emitted brilliant blue or green fluorescence with no penetration in the cell nuclei. Moreover, they were found to be in the cell membrane and in the cytoplasm. However, in a few cases, they show fluorescence near in cell membrane or even partial penetration in the nucleus. Some papers also include in vivo studies. For instance, Lin et al. studied the multimodal imaging potential of nanodiamond and gold hybrid nanoparticles ND@Au in Zebrafish embryos [397]. For in-vivo observation with FLIM of the ND distribution inside the zebrafish larvae, the embryos were given microinjections of ND@Au and were placed into a 3 mL embryo culture solution. For FLIM and X-ray tomography, 5-day-old embryos were prepared by the Golgi method. Images showed that ND@Au were quite eOLvident. Plus, they had intense emission which had significant contrast with the larvae autofluorescence. Likewise, Khan et al. investigated N,S-CDs for in vivo imaging in 4-week-old Kunming mice [392]. N,S-CDs are carefully injected into the mice with 200 μg/mL of the nanomaterial. The mice were examined for fluorescence after 30 min. Under the excitation wavelength of 488 nm bright fluorescence was observed at the bottom of the mice. The bright fluorescence was maintained after 50 min without any negative effects on the mice's health.

Apart from biocompatibility, stability is also one of the criteria for a promising bioimaging agent. For this reason, CBNs have also been tested for their photostability as well as their stability under different pH values. Data shows that CBNs, in general, display high photostability [261,390,398]. But when it comes to stability for pH, there was some quenching in strongly acidic and basic environments [391,399,400]. In other words, they appeared to have better stability in weakly acidic, weakly basic and neutral environments [396,401]. This variation of the PL properties can be attributed to the refilling or depletion of the valence bands through π-π* and n-π* transition in the graphene domains of CDs [396]. Aggregation of CBNs in strong acidic or basic media can be one of the reasons [391]. For example, for negatively charged CBNs highly acidic media can cause loss of surface charge which leads to agglomeration and instability of the material [400]. This problem can be solved by a modification that can protect harsh environments. This can be seen in the study conducted by [399] where they modified CDs with PAMAM dendrimers. Results showed that dendrimers screened the emission centers from the changes in their surrounding environment. Studies suggest that dendrimers, at low pH, behave as proton sponges and at high pH they can release immobilized protons present within their structure. Thus, they can act as a buffer system around CDs which protects them from the changes in pH.

A sensor is a tool that is used for the detection and quantification of the analyte in the sample. In general, a sensor consists of three components i.e. a receptor, a transducer, and a readout system [403]. A biosensor is a type of sensor where the receptor unit can recognize a biological target [404]. An ideal detector should satisfy requirements like the low limit of detection (LOD), a wide range of linearity, high sensitivity, high selectivity for the analyte, and high stability under different conditions. Besides, it should be able to provide good repeatability and reproducibility.

Among other biosensors, CBNs have grabbed great attention due to their top-notch physicochemical properties. CBNs such as graphene, carbon nanotubes and carbon dots are explored for their application as biosensors. As seen in Table 2 , the CBNs biosensors find applications for the detection of carbohydrates, amino acids, proteins, hormones, and viruses. It can be noticed that LOD is in atto-gram i.e. in the range of 10-18 g. The detection range provided by these biosensors is also very broad. For example, in a recent study, few-layer graphene (FLG) attached with 3-Polythiophene acetic acid (3-PTAA) nanoparticles were used as a label-free electrochemical immunosensor for the detection of alpha-fetoprotein (AFP). The dynamic range for AFP was found to be 0.0001–250 ng/mL and a detection limit of 0.047 pg/mL was reported [405]. Along the same lines, F. Chen et al. reported the preparation of chitosan-modified graphene field effective transistors (CTS-GFETs)which had anti-PCT immobilized on the graphene channel surface and investigated them for their biosensor application to detect procalcitonin (PCT) [406]. The PCT was detected by monitoring the change in VDirac of the devices as a result of the reaction between PCT and antibody in the channel. The PCT-antibody reaction was performed by introducing a particular concentration of PCT into the biosensor channel. After 120 min of incubation and washing of the channel was washed with PBS, Ids-Vgs curves were measured. The sensor showed a wide detection range of 1 ag/mL − 10 pg/mL with very low LOD of 0.82 ag/mL in PBS. The CTS-GFET biosensor also had high sensitivity showing 3 times higher sensitivity compared to bare GFET. Authors reported that on the modification of the CTS on the sensing channel surface, the protons and opposite ions in the vicinity of chitosan chains form an electric double layer (EDL) after a voltage is applied. The formation of EDL may have led to enhancement of the real capacitance values which could eventually result in better sensing performance [407].

As already mentioned, an apt sensor is not only very sensitive but also has very high selectivity for the target. Therefore, CBNs are also tested for their selectivity. For example, in the same study conducted by F. Chen et al. [406]. CTS-GFETs were also tested for their selectivity for PCT. The selectivity test was performed in the presence of Interleukin-6 (IL-6), C-reactive protein (CRP) and their mixture. The electrical signal was measured to determine the sensor response in the presence of interferents. The experiment was carried out in PBS in a neutral medium. The sensor had a relatively higher response for PCT. It was noticed that the high selectivity of the biosensing nano-system was connected to the high affinity of anti-PCT towards PCT. In fact, a similar pattern was observed in other studies. For example, Fan et al. synthesized ZIF-67@MoS2/rGO through a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method and combined the composite with the screen-printed electrode (SPE) to obtain visible light-responsive photoelectrochemical ZIF-67@MoS2/rGO biosensor [408]. The biosensor was prepared for the detection of glucose. The selectivity of ZIF-67@MoS2/rGO SPE for glucose (0.1 mM) was evaluated in the presence of 0.5 mM sucrose, maltose, fructose, lactose, uric acid, and NaCl. The photocurrent responses were recorded as biosensor responses. It was noticed that show that the sensor displayed good selectivity for glucose detection. Similarly, Sarker et al. found that their AC-mode GFET biosensors showed higher response for the analyte i.e. neuropeptide-Y (NPY) in the presence of the interferent namely interleukin-17 (IL-17) [409]. The validation of the selectivity of the sensor showed that it needed a bioreceptor to be selective towards the analyte. Moreover, a study conducted by Abdel-aal et al. indicated that concentration can also be a contributing factor in the selectivity of the sensor [410]. They noticed that their CDs-based biosensor for the detection of tryptophan showed a signal for tyrosine which was comparable to that for tryptophan. However, a low concentration of tyrosine had no significant effect on the sensing of the tryptophan.

Besides, other desirable sensor qualities CBNs also have high stability. Moreover, they give highly reproducible results with relative standard deviation (RSD) reaching as low as 0.48 %–2.27 % [411]. All these characteristics indicate that CBNs hold great potential in the field of biosensing. However, most of the studies oriented towards the diagnosis are conducted on serum as a sample. There is room for research on real blood samples. Moreover, these biosensors are still in the process of development and there is still a need for improvement, especially since many studies propose that the biosensors require miniaturization for practical application.

The application of carbon-based nanomaterials in catalysis far outweighs the conventional carbon materials (carbon black and activated carbon). This originates from the fact that, in carbon-based nanomaterials, phenomena such as catalyst deactivation and diffusion limitations are minimized [421]. Also, in the pursuit of developing a replacement for noble metals (e.g. Pt), a carbon-based metal-free catalyst, a more preferred, efficient, sustainable and promising solution to the dwindling supply of global energy and environmental challenges, has gained so much attention. Rigorous efforts are being channelled to harnessing the exceptional potential of carbon-based nanomaterials in catalysis e.g., in biocatalysis, acid-base catalysis, photocatalysis and in electrocatalysis [422–425]. Carbon nanomaterials applied in catalysis adopt diverse forms, ranging from carbon nanotubes, fullerenes, graphene, etc. Fig. 5 . Demonstrates the applications of CBNs in catalysis.

Fullerenes are carbon allotropes made up of sheets of graphene connected by pentagonal, hexagonal or heptagonal rings with each carbon atom being sp2 hybridized. Fullerene was discovered before CNTs and graphene, but its catalytic applications aren’t much compared to graphene and CNTs. This is attributed to the poor electronic conductivity and poor hydrophilic nature of this C60 and C70 [426–429].

However, much focus has been channelled towards investigating the uniqueness of fullerene that isn’t common to other allotropes of carbon [430–435] e.g., fullerene commonly exist as a molecule with a well define atomic and electronic structure, which can be applied to elucidate the structure–activity relationship [436–438;439]). Fullerenes undergo self-assembly into a microstructure or a nanostructure, which have the features of both homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysts [440–442]. Also, fullerenes are known to be excellent electron acceptors allowing them to modify the electronic structure of the species they interact with ([443,444]. In addition to this, the surface modification of fullerenes has led to the formation of quite a number of derivatives, which not only adopt the characteristics of the parent fullerene, but also, show enhanced physicochemical advantages like remarkable conductivity, appreciable hydrophilicity, and enhanced surface functionality [445]. Fullerenes have been applied in fields such as organic photovoltaics, pharmaceuticals, antioxidants, additives, water purification, biohazard protection, medical and catalysis etc. [446].

The application of fullerenes to the field of catalysis has been anticipated for a while with its promising and versatile nature, which is known to undergo the following pathways: as a catalyst, as a supporting medium for homogeneous catalysts or as a ligand. Fullerenes have been applied largely to catalytic organic reactions like pyrolysis of alkanes, alkyl and hydrogen transfer, hydrosilylation, and coupling reactions. Fullerenes have been reported to play the role of a catalyst in the cycloaddition reaction of dimethyl iminodiacetate to maleimides through photolysis to form 2,5-dimethoxycarbonyl pyrrolidine derivatives [446]. Also, with singlet oxygen, fullerenes in a minute quantity can undergo Diels-Alder’s reaction in the presence of visible or UV lights to form a photo-oxygenated product [446]. Fullerenes have been applied as a carbon nanomaterial to hydrogenation as a catalyst, which can activate hydrogen gas in the presence of ultraviolet radiation and serve as a catalyst in the hydrogenation process of nitrobenzene to aniline with high yield and selectivity. The catalytic hydrogenation of aromatic nitro compounds by fullerenes was graphically demonstrated and explained in [447], where 4-nitrobenzonitrile was catalyzed by C60 as a catalyst. It was found that 100 % nitrobenzonitrile yield was achieved within an hour, 100 % selectivity was obtained and no bis(cyano) azoxybenzene was observed to remain in the final products. Fig. 6 . Shows the conversion of nitrobenzene into aniline by fullerene.

The modification of fullerene has paved the way for abundant fullerene derivatives. Similar to the main fullerene, the derivatives also have a defined molecular structure. However, different from the main fullerene, the electronic structures of the derivatives are prone to localized rearrangement, which is necessary for activating the noble carbon cage and tailoring the active sites for catalysis. For example, to induce the catalytic activity of carbon, N-doping is employed as an effective strategy. However, the question is, which type of N is the main activity contributor is yet to be proffered an answer [448–450] Fullerene derivatives having specific N configurations are good tools needed to solve the issue of catalytic actions of differently configured N-dopants. Conversely, oxidized carbon nanomaterials show potential catalytic activity in two-electron ORR to produce H2O2, but the controversial question is, which type of O-containing functional group is the actual site for catalysis [451,452]. Surface functionalized fullerene with targeted O-containing moieties can therefore be applied in elucidating the activity of different O-containing groups. To achieve this, neither graphene nor CNTs can be used to accomplish this task owing to their high structural complexity. In addition to these, with the advancement in recognition of the key catalytic roles played by the defects in the structure, fullerene is envisaged to be a better type of precursor in the construction of metal-free carbon nanomaterial electrocatalysts with high porosity and abundant defects.

The groundbreaking discovery of CNTs by Lijima in 1991, set a new pace for the chemistry of carbon. Since its discovery, ardent research has been carried out and is still ongoing. Structurally, CNTs are divided into single-wall carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) and multi-wall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) [453]. The keen interest placed on the chemistry of CNTs by researchers reflects its uniqueness. Properties such as high aspect ratios, surface areas, remarkable chemical and thermal stability, excellent electronic, optical and mechanical properties etc. [454].

In the past, certain studies have shown that CNTs can promote the transfer of electrons and augment electrochemical reactivity [455,456]. Also, the application of CNTs for enzyme-catalyzing biosensors has been reviewed [457]. Numerous publications have reported on the use of CNTs as catalysts for various processes, such as the oxidation of aniline and H2S, esterification, decomposition of methane, oxidative dehydrogenation of propane and ethylbenzene, catalytic wet air oxidation of phenol and hydroxylation ([456,458].

In 2013, it was demonstrated that, due to the π-π interactions between peroxides, radicals, and the grapheme sheets of CNTs, CNTs can catalyze the aerobic oxidation of ethylbenzene to acetophenone in the liquid phase [459]. In another study, it was shown that chemical vapor-deposited multi-wall carbon nanotubes MWCNTs can catalyze C6H12 aerobic oxidation in the liquid phase, which showed high activity and selectivity [460]. Coupled with these findings, MWCNTs possess high catalytic activity in oxidative dehydrogenation (ODH) of dihydroanthracene under controlled and minimized reaction conditions without utilizing surface-loaded metals [461]. Also, Zhang et al. were able to synthesize surface-modified carbon nanotubes CNTs, which were proven to be a good catalyst for the oxidative dehydrogenation of n-butanes to butenes [462]. This study further proves that the selectivity of CNTs for C4 alkenes, specifically butadiene, was enhanced when compared to a metal catalyst.

Recently, Chesnokov et al. investigated the decomposition of formic acid in the presence of carbon nanotubes CNTs to produce pure hydrogen gas. In this study, carbon nanotubes CNTs catalyzed the decomposition of formic acid generating preferably, hydrogen and carbon (IV) oxide. The research reported that the structural features of carbon nanomaterials affect their activity in the decomposition process of formic acid. In addition, the treatment of carbon nanotubes CNTs with an acid or a base enhances its activity towards the catalytic decomposition of formic acid, with the alkali treatment, being able to amplify the activity more than the acid treatment does. The treatment with either an acid or a base has different, but a fundamental impact on the selectivity of the catalytic decomposition of formic acid. Treatment with alkali causes an increase towards the selectivity of hydrogen and CO2 but a considerable decrease was observed upon acid treatment [463]. Fig. 7 presents the decomposition of formic acid with CNTs.

Graphene is an allotrope of carbon which is a monolayer of hexagonally arranged carbon atoms having a large theoretical surface area, measured to be about 2630 m2g−1 [119,464]. Graphene has gained much attention in catalytic applications, due to its large surface-to-volume ratio. The catalytic activity of a conventionally used catalyst can be regulated by its surface-to-volume ratio. Graphene with a 2D structure fulfils this attribute. In addition, graphene’s extremely high thermal conductivity and high chemical stability can enhance the loading of active sites within the catalytic framework. Graphene’s high electrical activity makes it suitable for electrochemical processes [465].

Consequently, an important feature to focus on is the distinct atomic and electronic structure of graphene which makes it unique and applicable in the field of catalysis. The chemical property of graphene readily responds to changes in the chemical environment or matrix. Graphene has no band gap (zero) and the density of state (DOS) around the Fermi level Ef is very low. The readily and easily permeable structure on both sides of graphene sheets would, to a great extent decrease the time taken for reactants to migrate to an active site via diffusion. Transfer of reactant material becomes facile without internal diffusion, thus accelerating the rate of reaction in heterogeneous catalytic processes primarily determined by mass transfer or diffusion [466].

The sites on the surface of graphene are grouped into three, these are; the top (T), bridge (B) and hollow (H) sites [467–469]. Concerning density functional theory (DFT), adatoms from precious metals, like Ti, Ni, Ru, and Co and V, are adsorbed with stability on the hollow sites. Conversely, transition elements and non-metals with partially or fully occupied d shells, such as Pt, Cu, and Pd, are very stable on the bridge sites. In contrast, Ag, Au, I, Cl, H, F and Br are very stable on the Top (T) sites [467,468]. Depending on the type of adsorption, adatoms are classified into either long-range, weak Van der Waals adsorption (Physisorption) or short-range, strong covalent adsorption (chemisorption), also based on the properties of both adsorbent and adsorbate [467,468].

Pristine graphene, which is chemically unreactive, was shown to have small adsorption energy and limited transfer of charge for the simulated adsorptions of various clusters and gaseous molecules on it [470–473]. A major problem in catalysis involving graphene is discovering a good, and practical means of activating it. Introduction of defects i.e., the creation of vacancies or doping with foreign species [474–477] alternatively, doping the substrate underneath epitaxial graphene [478,479]. During doping, graphene may function as the catalytic center. However, the chemical inertness of graphene does not imply a limitation [480,481].

In solid-state catalysis, graphene plays 3 distinct roles, namely, as a catalytic center, as a support, or as a cover. Graphene has been investigated to be an excellent support for catalysts due to the highly tunable interaction between graphene and the supported catalysts.

Furthermore, graphene has a remarkable facility for a wide range of functionalities and shows good potential as a novel class of carbo-catalyst [482]. Graphene was utilized as a mild and effective carbon catalyst for aldehydes and ketones formation from different alcohols, alkenes, and alkynes. Loh et al. made a review on carbon catalysts, which focused on GO and its derivatives [483]. Carboxylic acids, spin electrons, aromatic CH, and quinones are some important groups and entities that can be found at the edges or defect sites [484], while oxygen-bearing-functional groups can be introduced onto the aromatic framework in GO. Also, the amphiphilic behavior of GO contributes to its preeminent phase-transfer catalyst in oilwater biphasic systems. Zhu and Donge (2013) also pointed out advances in graphene-based electrocatalysts and their use inelectrochemicalenergydevices like fuel cells and batteries [485].

One of the most important reactions occurring in systems involving the conversion of energy and biological processes is the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) [486]. Particularly, in the advancement of technologies involving transformation or conversions of energy, polymer-based electrolyte membrane high-performing fuel cells with low emissions have been investigated thoroughly and recognized as a central aspect of electrochemical technology. A polymer electrolyte fuel cell is made up of a separated cathode and an anode electrode in either an acid or alkaline electrolyte membrane. In the oxidation of fuel, hydrogen has been oxidized at the anode, simultaneously, the oxidation–reduction reaction ORR occurs at the cathode. The oxygen reduction reaction proceeds via two routes. The first route involves the reduction of four electrons which leads to the formation of water H2O. In the second route, hydrogen peroxide is generated and is finally reduced to H2O [487]. The first route is more likely to occur as it transfers four electrons per molecule [486]. Conversely, the H2O2 generated in the second, the two-electron, route reduces the stability through the deterioration of the membrane and ionomer [488]. Consequently, it reduces the catalytic effect by losing active sites. This is a fact for precious-metal-based active sites which undergo oxidative attacks [489].

There are two major problems encountered in the application of polymer electrolyte membrane fuel cells, these are; high cost and short-lived (low durability) of the cathode oxygen reduction reactions ORR catalysts. The cathode ORR catalysts are generally composed of Platinum is commonly used as a cathode in oxygen reduction reactions. Sometimes, other rare metals which are expensive are used, these account for nearly 50 % of the cost of materials in fuel cells [490]. Platinum-based cathode catalysts are the most used in fuel cells as they are more efficient [491]. However, they also suffer from limitations like catalyst deactivation [486]. Owing to this, fuel cell application has been limited by the cost of maintenance and capital. Graphene is a promising alternative and is continuously being explored to combat this problem. Graphene is usually incorporated as a support for Pt or alloys of Pt catalysts to create a large electrochemical surface area ECSA. Consequently, by incorporating a large ECSA, the diffusion of on the surface of the metal sites is further enhanced and accelerated, thus increasing the efficiency of ORR. This has been demonstrated in an experiment where the operating condition of reduction current on Pt/RG-O is set at 4.94 A/g. This is significantly high as compared to 3.17 A/g on Pt/C carried out under kinetic control at a polarized potential of 0.6 V [492].

Materials of carbon are highly significant in catalysis, specifically as catalytic supports, as a result of their abundance, mechanical stability and high surface area, [493]. Carbon, due to its abundance, can be obtained from waste [494]. Once catalysts lose their activity, materials of carbon offer the benefit of easy recovery of the active phase. Electrospun carbon nanofibers can be produced easily by using various polymers, forming in fibers with a wide range of properties and sizes [495,496]. These fibers, having diameters between those of traditional carbon fibers and carbon nanotubes, are expedient in the field of catalysis, catalytic supports, and platforms for other fibers. Furthermore, they find applications in electrochemical energy storage and biosensing devices.

Carbon nanofibers (CNFs) are largely applied in catalysis, particularly as supports in electrocatalysis beneficial for fuel cells, batteries and water-splitting systems [497]. Fibrous electrodes, when compared to conventional catalyst powders, have more advantages, such as their binding and non-agglomerating nature. [498], reviewed the applications of fibrous frameworks, with CNFs, for the electrocatalysis of oxygen. Electrospun fibers well well-studied. Electrospun CNFs are good support systems for precious metals in electrocatalysis [497]. [499] developed Pd-Ce catalysts on electrospun CNFs applied in direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs), demonstrating remarkable catalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of CBNs in environmental remediation
ce:section.ce:para: The treatment of wastewater has been done by several methods. Among the methods mentioned above, adsorption has garnered considerable attention in wastewater remediation [500,501]. Therefore, the following section emphasizes the application of various CBNs in the adsorption process for removing different pollutants from wastewater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CBNs in organic pollutants removal
ce:section.ce:para: Organic pollutants have been considered as emerging pollutants that can cause adverse effects on the environment worldwide. The discharge of these pollutants without treatment can have detrimental effects on living organisms and the entire ecosystem. Besides, organic pollutants have more complex structures, which provide them resistance against biodegradation [502]. Recently, CBNs has the attention to removing organic pollutants from water bodies. This section will review the adsorption of organic pollutants onto CBNs.

The process includes the transfer of gas or liquid solutes (adsorbates) onto the surface of a solid (adsorbent), creating a molecular or atomic film called adsorption. Adsorption is an eco-friendly and versatile for removing organic and inorganic pollutants from wastewater because of its high efficacy, ease of operation, and low expenses [503].

Carbon nanotubes have been found to exhibit robust adsorption capabilities for polar organic compounds owing to their distinctive structure. This structure enables potent interactions with organic compounds through non-covalent forces such as hydrogen bonding, π–π stacking, electrostatic forces, van der Waals forces, and hydrophobic interactions [504,505]. Understanding the adsorption mechanism of organic pollutants onto carbon nanotubes is complicated. The capability to remove pollutants depends on the interaction between the pollutants and the nanotubes [506,507]. The removal of organic pollutants from water onto carbon nanotubes is also affected by the active site, surface area, purity, and the functional groups present at their surfaces. The controlled pore size distribution in parallel with the presence of abundant active sites at their surface provided CNTs with incomparable sorption efficacy [508].

Additionally, CNT’s tend to aggregate in aqueous solutions, creating significant interstitial spaces and grooves that provide numerous adsorption sites, thereby enhancing organic materials adsorption. MWCNT’s are particularly effective in removing organic compounds, including methyl orange, arsenazo(III), and methyl red from wastewater. Similarly, SWCNT’s exhibit high adsorption efficiency for organic pollutants due to their large surface area and substantial micropore volume [506]. The regeneration ability is a crucial factor in determining the CNT’s economical viability. Reng et al. (2017) proposed a method for regenerating CNT’s by reducing the pH of the solution using nitric acid [505].

Many studies have mentioned that the functionalization of CBNs contributes to improving the adsorption capacity by increasing the number of active sites at the adsorbent surface. According to Zhang et al. (2011), who investigated the removal of sulfamethoxazole from aqueous solution onto functionalized CNT’s, the hydrophobic, electrostatic, π–π interactions, and hydrogen bonds were the main contributors in the removal process. Accordingly, an enhancement in the adsorption capacity was expected as a result of MWCNT’s functionalization [509]. In the same way, the adsorption of bisphenol A (BPA) onto CNT’s/Fe3O4 magnetic nanocomposites prepared by hydrothermal method, was attributed to the π–π interactions between the CNT’s and the benzene ring in BPA molecules [510]. Ncibi and Sillanpää (2015) studied the removal of two antibiotics, Oxytetracycline (OXY) and Ciprofloxacin (CIP) from aqueous solution by SWCNT’s and MWCNT’s. The adsorption capacities for OXY were 559 and 507 mg/g for SWCNT’s and MWCNT’s, respectively. The SWCNT’s have a CIP adsorption capacity of 724 mg/g and MWCNT’s have 605 mg/g [511]. Álvarez-Torrellas and colleagues (2016) utilized the MWCNT’s in tetracycline removal from the aqueous solution, where the adsorption capacity reached 192 mg/g [512]. In another study, the removal abilities of pristine and hydroxylated MWCNTs against the sulfamethazine were examined, and the adsorption capacities were 24.78 and 13.31 mg/g, respectively [513]. In fact, the literature has detailed information and illustrations of the adsorption of organic pollutants using CBNs [508,514].

For the same purpose, graphene and its derivatives have also been utilized. Due to its cost-effectiveness in production, graphene is considered an ideal adsorbent for water and wastewater remediation [508]. The adsorption capacity of graphene-based nanomaterials varies depending on the adsorbate type (ionic or hydrophobic). Graphene, as one of the CBNs, exhibits a high specific surface area and outstanding chemical stability, rendering it highly effective for adsorbing organic pollutants. However, van der Waals interactions between adjacent graphene sheets in water cause them to aggregate, reducing the surface area and, consequently, the adsorption efficiency. Thus, appropriate modification of graphene is necessary to mitigate this limitation [515,516].

Modifying the surface area of graphene with specific functional groups (Fig. 8 ) is crucial as it enhances the interaction area between graphene and pollutants, leading to higher efficiencies. The hydrophobicity of the pristine graphene hinders its dispersion properly in water. This challenge was overcome by adding distinct functional groups by chemically surface modifying [517]. For instance, functionalizing graphene into GO via the Hummer method introduces copious functional groups, including epoxide, hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl, and disrupts the conjugation in the graphene plane [518]. GO can effectively adsorb a wide range of pollutants due to its oxygen-containing groups. Ramesha et al. (2011) examined the adsorption ability of GO nanomaterial toward cations and anions. Accordingly, the removal efficiency in the cationic compounds was higher [519]. The existence of numerous functional groups and the strong acidity of the GO increased the basic compounds' adsorption efficacy. In contrast, graphene has a hydrophobic surface and demonstrates high organic matter adsorption through sturdy π–π interactions [520].

Many researchers have utilized graphene-based nanomaterials in the removal of different organic pollutants. For instance, ciprofloxacin was removed by porous graphene hydrogel [521], KOH-activated graphene [522], and Single layer GO [523]. The adsorption capacities were 235.6, 194.6, and 379 mg/g, respectively. Bradder et al. (2010) successfully employed the Hummers–Offeman method to oxidize graphite. The graphite oxide (GO) was subsequently used in the adsorption of dye from aqueous solutions. While graphite oxide (GO) did not exhibit significant changes in surface area, its structure expanded, and numerous functional groups were attached, enhancing the GO adsorption efficacy. The results indicated that the adsorption of several dyes, such as methylene blue and malachite green was much higher in GO than on graphite. The adsorption numbers fit well with the Langmuir isotherm, showing an adsorption capacity of 351 mg/g for GO and 248 mg/g for graphite. Additionally, the presence of negatively charged functional groups on the surface of the adsorbent, the adsorption mechanism was attributed to the electrostatic attraction [524]. In another research, Zhao and Liu (2009) employed the modified expanded graphite (MEG) powder in the adsorption of methylene blue (MB) dye. The maximum adsorption capacity was obtained at higher pH values, initial concentrations, and temperatures. The experimental data align closely with the pseudo-second-order kinetic model, while the adsorption data conform to the Langmuir adsorption model, indicating an adsorption capacity of 7.77 μg/g [525].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CBNs in inorganic pollutants removal
ce:section.ce:para: The existence of inorganic materials in the aqua system has been classified as one of the most urgent topics in the world, especially the heavy metals that can accumulate in the surrounding environment and cause several serious health problems, even at low concentrations. Therefore, the removal of heavy metals has grabbed attention. Recently, CBNs have attracted significant attention for their use as adsorbents in heavy metal removal because of their ample surface active site and well-defined pore size distribution [526]. Graphene-based materials and CNTs have been extensively employed for the heavy metals removal from wastewater owing to their expansive surface area, efficient manufacturing, adjustable surface chemistry, non-corrosive characteristics, and the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on their surfaces [527].

The adsorption of heavy metals by graphene-based nanomaterials has been extensively achieved because they have a relatively large surface area. However, graphene is considered insoluble and difficult to disperse because of its strong van der Waals force. These properties hinder the adsorption process of heavy metals. Thus, the modification of graphene has occurred widely to overcome graphene limitations. GO has several properties, such as being more hydrophilic, larger surface area, and containing a considerable number of oxygen-containing atoms in different forms (epoxy, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups). These entities make GO favorable for heavy metal adsorption [528].

Many researchers have utilized graphene-based nanomaterials in heavy metal adsorption. In this context, Sitko et al. (2013) examined the GO in the removal of Zn(II), Cu(II), Cd(II), Pb(II), Cr(VI), and U(VI). The adsorption capacities for the above heavy metals were 246, 117.5, 530, 842, 232.55, and 299 mg/g, respectively [528]. In another study, Pb(II) and Cd(II) were removed by GO/Fe3O4 composite, where the adsorption capacities reached 38.5 mg/g for Pb(II) and 4.4 mg/g for Cd(II) [529]. Maji et al. (2018) synthesized a novel GO@iron-aluminium oxide composite for arsenic(III) removal. The adsorption efficiency and capacity reached 95 % and 42.2 mg/g, respectively. The adsorption of arsenic(III) onto the GO@iron-aluminium oxide composite was found to follow pseudo-first-order and Langmuir isotherm [530]. Huong and colleagues (2018) functionalized manganese ferrite/GO nanocomposites for As(V) adsorption from aqueous solution. Accordingly, the removal efficacy was almost 100 %, and the maximum adsorption capacity of 240.4 mg/g. The adsorption of As(V) followed the pseudo-second-order model [531]. In another study, nanohybrids of tetraethylenepentamine and GO decorated with MnFe2O4 magnetic nanoparticles (TEPA-GO/MnFe2O4) were prepared. The composite was utilized in the adsorption of Pb(II). The TEPA-GO/MnFe2O4 showed an adsorption capacity of 263.2 mg/g, fitted with the Langmuir isotherm and pseudo-second-order, and the adsorption process was found to be endothermic and spontaneous [532]. Liao et al. (2020) synthesized GO-MnFe2O4 composites for Pb(II) and Cu(II) removal. Langmuir model and the pseudo-second-order model described the adsorption of Pb(II) and Cu(II) onto GO-MnFe2O4. Also, the adsorption capacities for Pb(II) and Cu(II) onto GO-MnFe2O4 composites were calculated to be 263.85 and 103.41 mg/g, respectively [533].

Carbon nanotubes have also been utilized to adsorb heavy metal ions from the aqueous solutions. Despite the numerous studies reporting the adsorption of heavy metals by CNTs, the applicability of CNTs, in reality, is hampered due to the absence of carboxylate, hydroxyl, sulfate, phosphate, amide, amino groups, and other surface functional groups [534]. To overcome this issue, Lata and Samadder (2016) demonstrated that carbon nanotubes can be oxidized by HNO3, NaClO, and KMnO4 solutions [535]. To improve uptake efficiency, the modification of carbon nanotubes should be tailored to the targeted specific pollutant. Enhancing the surface of CNT’s by introducing new functional groups can significantly boost their adsorption capacities for metal ions by increasing the number of active sites available for adsorption. According to Zhang et al. (2016), oxidization, chemical grafting, and physical methods were used in the surface modification of CNTs [536]. In this context, several researchers have evaluated the modified CNTs in heavy metal removal. Anitha et al. (2015) studied the adsorption mechanism of distinct heavy metals Cd(II), Cu(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II) by the functionalized SWCNTs with functional groups –COO(−), –OH, and –CONH2. According to their findings, the adsorption capacities for all targeted metals were significantly improved for all functional groups. However, the functional groups’ efficiencies varied broadly. For instance, the functionalized CNTs with COO(−) group enhanced the adsorption by 150–230 % compared with bare CNTs, whereas the functionalized CNTs with OH and-CONH2 groups raised the efficiency by only 10–47 %. Also, the adsorption efficiency of each metal differed for the same adsorbent. Regarding adsorption efficiency, the heavy metals can be sorted decently as Pb(II), Cu(II), Cd(II), and then Hg(II) [537]. Mubarak et al. (2016) synthesized MWCNTs via microwave chemical vapor deposition and used them in Pb(II) adsorption. Accordingly, the maximum adsorption capacity was 104.2 mg/g, and the maximum removal efficiency (99.9 %) was achieved at a pH value of 5 after 22.5 min and for an initial concentration of 10 mg/L [538]. In another study, Kończyk and colleagues (2019) prepared MWCNTs and functionalized them seleno-phosphoryl group. The produced MWCNTs were employed in Pb(II) adsorption from the aqueous solution. The optimum conditions were pH 5, a metal concentration of 20 mg/L, a temperature of 313 K, and a contact time of 60 min. Thermodynamic analysis revealed that Pb(II) ions were spontaneously physisorbed onto the prepared MWCNTs during an endothermic process [539]. Mercury (II) ions were also adsorped from an aqueous solution using polydopamine-decorated SWCNTs. The results of this study demonstrated that the prepared adsorbent is more efficient at basic pH levels for removing Hg(II). The isotherm and kinetic studies showed that the adsorption of Hg(II) onto the polydopamine-decorated SWCNTs follows the Freundlich model and pseudo-second-order equation [540]. Fard et al. (2022) designed ethylenediamine-surface-modified single-walled carbon nanotubes (EDA-SWCNTs) and utilized them in lead and cadmium removal. The maximum removal efficiencies reached 96.91 % for lead ions and 93.47 % for cadmium ions. Equilibrium isotherm analysis showed a strong fit with the Langmuir model, indicating homogeneous monolayer adsorption of lead and cadmium onto the CNT’s [541].

E-textiles, also known as electronic textiles, provide conductive capabilities while preserving the inherent characteristics of textile materials. To facilitate the expansion of E-textiles, it is necessary to create functional materials and increase the scale of manufacturing techniques. The porous and anisotropic three-dimensional nature of textiles necessitates the development of scalable techniques to effectively use these materials. Functional materials based on organic carbon are extensively used due to their cost-effective production procedure and widespread accessibility [542], Lately, carbon-based conductive materials like graphene, GO, rGO, CNTs, CB, and AC have gained prominence over metallic particles. This shift is attributed to their superior electrical properties, extraordinary mechanical stability, and electrical performance, which can improve the mechanical strength and durability of textiles. These properties are vital for protective clothing and other applications requiring robust material performance [543]. Typically, conductive inks and printing pastes are prepared from material suspension in liquid solvents, which are then applied to textiles using a variety of application processes [544]. Fig. 9 shows the application of CBNs in E-textile.

The heating performance of a dip-coated heating device based on CNTs was found to be good. Upon the application of a voltage of 40 V, a little heater attained a temperature of 50 °C for 200 s. However, its capacity for heating diminished after a continuous duration of 220 s [545]. An alternative method used included the utilization of CB screen-printed heaters on polyester cloth. The incorporation of more layers of CB print paste resulted in a decrease in fabric resistance, thereby enhancing the Joule heating efficiency. The application of screen-printed layers resulted in the formation of a compact layer of conductive material on the fabric, hence enabling enhanced charge mobility in comparison to the dip-coated technique. Additionally, the heaters printed with CB screen not only exhibited superior heat generating capabilities compared to heaters based on CNTs, but also demonstrated enhanced endurance, retaining performance after numerous heating cycles lasting up to 60 min. The screen-printed layers are responsible for the decreased resistance and better charge mobility, which contribute to this advantage. Also, by adding a polyurethane (PU) matrix to the CB screen-printed heaters, their flexibility and stretchability are enhanced, rendering them well-suited for cost-effective, wearable, and stretchy heating purposes [546].

Carbon-based fabric sensors canchange their electrical characteristics in reaction to chemical environments. The efficacy of these sensors is attributed to the intrinsic ability of carbon particles to adsorb gaseous molecules, making them well-suited for the detection of chemicals such as acetone and methane vapor. Compared to other varieties, these sensors are less often reported in the literature [547].

The sensor was produced by screen printing method, including carbon ink consisting of graphene pellets and a 3 % weight aqueous dispersion of multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs). The sensitivity of the sensor is assessed by evaluating the variations in relative resistance before and aftergas exposure, which serves as an indicator of the gas concentration [548].

There are a lot of benefits of carbon-based electrodes. In the fabrication of amperometry sensors, carbon-based conductive electrodes are favored over metal electrodes due to their inherent stability and ability to withstand oxidation and reduction in chemical solutions. As a result, they are more suited for extended chemical sensing applications.

The sensor has particular use in the detection of NADH, H2O2, and ferrocyanide levels in the sweat of the wearer, suggesting its potential utility in the field of health monitoring [548]. The sensor is engineered to endure mechanical strain, making it suitable for regular use. The use of screen-printing technology in the manufacturing process guarantees the attainment of reliability, repeatability, and cost-effective mass production.

The screen-printing technique offers a high degree of freedom in sensor design, enabling the customization of forms, sizes, and functionalization using biological components and nanomaterials. Nevertheless, there is a lack of a clear definition of the criteria for chemical identification and concentration determination in fabric-based sensors, suggesting a need for more advancements in this field [542].

There are many commonly used energy storage technologies, such as flexible supercapacitors, electrical double-layer capacitors, and batteries. Energy captured from sources such as piezo-resistors, solar cells, and fuel cells is stored by these devices. The devices consist of three fundamental components, namely electrodes, electrolytes, and separators [549]. The focus is on the scalable production of flexible electrodes employing CBNs. This is because these materials possess inherent specific capacitance and a large surface area [550]. The incorporation of carbon compounds into textile substrates leads to an increase in surface area and roughness, hence enhancing charge accommodation. Dip coating, screen printing, and inkjet printing are widely used manufacturing techniques for the application of carbonaceous compounds onto textiles. The use of metallic active materials, such as MnO2 nanoflakes, RuO2, and Co(OH)2, on textiles like cotton has been explored as a means to improve specific capacitance. Screen printing silk fabric using MnO2-coated hollow carbon microspheres obviates the need for further coatings [551]. The electrochemical stability of MWCNT dip-coated supercapacitors on cotton fabric treated with CoCl2 solution is seen to be high, indicating their favorable characteristics. The supercapacitors composed of inkjet-printed PVA gel/SWNT and RuO2 nanowire/SWCNT exhibit improved performance, characterized by elevated Coulombic efficiency, specific capacitance, power density, and energy density [552,553].

Additionally, it has been shown that polyaniline, when coated with GO, has enhanced efficacy in obstructing X-ray beams when compared to shields without GO. The attenuation efficiency of a 20 kV X-ray beam was found to be 9.14 % when poly(vinylidene) fluoride homopolymer (PVDF)/barium sulfate was used. However, the inclusion of 4.0 wt% of a GO nanosheet resulted in an enhanced attenuation efficiency of 24.65 % [554]. According to Fujimori et al., CNTs have superior X-ray attenuation capabilities compared to pyrolytic graphite and fullerenes [555].

Zarei et al. successfully developed an X-ray radiation shield by integrating polyaniline and hybrid GO nanoflakes with tungsten-bismuth-tin. Their findings validated the efficacy of this material and indicated its potential use in the production of radiation-resistant garments [556].

In summary, it can be inferred that smart textiles including carbon nanoparticles can respond to various environmental stimuli, including changes in temperature or exposure to chemicals. The inclusion of this feature is of utmost importance in the development of protective apparel that can effectively adjust to perilous surroundings. The research on smart textiles and their applications emphasizes the ability of nanoparticle-embedded fabrics to respond to environmental stimuli.

Even though CBNs have geared the researchers' attention, they are still facing several challenges that should be overcome in future. The limitations and challenges of CBNs include scalability and cost-effectiveness, toxicity and environmental concerns, integration and commercialization prospects, and emerging trends with potential applications. This section will provide a brief description of each challenge.

Achieving scalability in the production of carbon-based nanocomposites is a significant challenge. While laboratory-scale synthesis methods often yield promising results, transitioning to large-scale industrial production introduces several difficulties. Techniques like chemical vapor deposition (CVD) for graphene and arc discharge for CNTs struggle with maintaining consistent quality, uniformity, and cost-effectiveness on a larger scale. These challenges hinder the widespread adoption of these materials in commercial applications, underscoring the need for improved synthesis methods that can reliably produce high-quality nanocomposites at an industrial scale[557]. Thus, researchers should be directed toward developing a scalable process to meet the raised demand for the CBNs.

Additionally, the preparation of high-quality nano-sized carbon materials on a large scale is considered a resource-intensive approach, which affects its economic viability. The high cost of production remains a critical obstacle to CBNs adoption in commercial applications [558]. To ensure effective integration into industrial sectors, the synthesis process must be cost-effective while maintaining quality. Efforts are underway to optimize production methods, use more affordable precursors, and explore novel synthesis techniques that could lower costs while maintaining the desired material properties. Achieving a balance between quality and cost-effectiveness is essential for making these nanocomposites a practical option for a wider array of applications [559].

The potential toxicity of CBNs remains a critical concern, particularly in applications that involve human exposure. Despite their exceptional properties, such as strength and conductivity, there are ongoing apprehensions about their effects on human health. Extensive research has been conducted to assess the biocompatibility and toxicological risks of CNTs and graphene. These studies examine variables such as the size, shape, and surface functionalization of the nanomaterials, as these factors significantly influence how they interact with biological systems and affect cellular processes [560].

While various studies indicate that some types of CBNs have relatively low toxicity, thorough toxicological assessments are essential to develop safe usage guidelines. Long-term studies are crucial for understanding the potential bioaccumulation of CBNs and their effects over extended periods. This is especially important in fields like biomedicine, where prolonged exposure or accumulation in tissues could pose health risks. It is also essential in consumer products that come into regular contact with humans.

Alongside health concerns, the environmental impact of CBNs production and disposal has become increasingly significant. The persistence and bioaccumulation of these materials in ecosystems could have unintended consequences for wildlife and biodiversity. Addressing these environmental concerns requires the development of more sustainable manufacturing processes, including eco-friendly synthesis methods and materials that are biodegradable or easier to recycle. Furthermore, it is essential to create responsible disposal strategies that minimize long-term environmental damage. As the use of CBNs expands, the industry must prioritize these sustainability efforts, ensuring that growth in their production and application does not come at the expense of environmental well-being. Taking proactive measures in recycling and waste management is essential for reducing the ecological impact of advanced materials [17].

Integrating CBNs into commercial applications faces various limitations, with regulatory approval being a key factor in determining their potential for widespread use. As these materials are still relatively new and their long-term effects on human health and the environment are not fully understood, regulatory agencies have stringent requirements for testing and validation before they can be used in consumer products or industrial processes. Incorporating these nanocomposites into complex systems presents challenges, particularly in securing compatibility with other matters and preserving the integrity of the outcome [561].

For successful commercialization, CBNs must comply with safety, environmental, and performance standards established by regulatory bodies based on their intended applications. The approval processes can be lengthy and costly, often requiring extensive data on toxicity, biodegradability, and the overall behavior of the material in real-world conditions. Therefore, strong interdisciplinary research and collaboration are essential.
