By facilitating knowledge exchange and collaboration between experts from various fields, researchers can accelerate the development of practical applications for carbon-based nanocomposites. Furthermore, it is essential to establish standardized testing protocols for nanomaterials and to gain regulatory clarity across various markets to promote their widespread adoption. Once regulatory barriers are overcome, the full potential of carbon-based nanocomposites in industries like electronics, energy storage, healthcare, and manufacturing can be realized, fostering innovation and economic growth[562].

To sum up, CBNs belong to a versatile category of nanomaterials that possess distinct properties. This diverse group of nanomaterials consists of fullerenes, graphene, carbon nanotubes, nanodiamonds, carbon nanofibers, carbon dots and their derivatives.
•
There are mainly three different approaches to synthesize them: chemical synthesis, physical synthesis and green synthesis. Chemical synthesis includes decomposition reactions and chemical vapor deposition. Chemical synthesis is used to synthesize graphene, carbon nanotubes and carbon fibers. Meanwhile, mechanical milling, laser ablation and arc discharge are the methods involved in physical synthesis. Graphene flakes, fullerenes and carbon nanotubes can be obtained through this approach. Green synthesis is applied for the reduction of GO to graphene and to obtain luminescent carbon nanoparticles.
•
CBNs are mainly characterized with the help of spectroscopic techniques. UV–visible spectroscopy, IR and XPS are suitable for CNTs and graphene. Raman spectroscopy is useful for graphene identification and determination of its layer number etc. HRTEM can be applied to distinguish between SWCNTs and MWCNTs. Identification of fullerenes and SWCNTs can be achieved through HRTEM-EDX.
•
Biomedical applications of CBNs are primarily related to drug delivery, bioimaging and biosensing. In general, they are not toxic with cell viability around 80 %. These nanomaterials can act as pH-responsive drug delivery systems that can interact with drugs through π-π stacking, hydrogen bonding and electrostatic forces. They show a variety of drug release mechanisms including diffusion, swelling and relaxations of the carrier. They can show excitation-dependent and independent emission behaviors. Among CBNs, carbon dots (CDs) are more prominent for their bioimaging applications. They mostly remain photostable but can experience quenching with pH variations. As biosensors, they can be used to detect carbohydrates, amino acids, proteins, hormones, and viruses with LOD in the order of 10-18 g.
•
CBNs catalysts can minimize problems like catalyst deactivation and diffusion limitations. Among them, fullerenes can be applied for the pyrolysis of alkanes, alkyl and hydrogen transfer, hydrosilylation, and coupling reactions. Oxidation of aniline and H2S, esterification, decomposition of methane, oxidative dehydrogenation of propane and ethylbenzene, catalytic wet air oxidation of phenol and hydroxylation are some examples of utilization of CNTs as catalysts. Meanwhile, graphene is commonly employed as a support for Pt or its alloys to increase surface area. Electrocatalysis in fuel cells, batteries and water-splitting systems can benefit from CNFs as support.
•
Both graphene and CNTs can facilitate the removal of organic compounds. CNTs can interact with pollutants through non-covalent forces. MWCNTs are promising for the removal of distinct organic pollutants. In addition, Graphene-based materials and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) have been extensively employed for the heavy metals removal from wastewater owing to their expansive surface area, efficient manufacturing, adjustable surface chemistry, non-corrosive characteristics, and the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on their surfaces.
•
Graphene, GO, rGO, CNTs, CB and AC with their excellent electrical property and mechanical stability can improve the mechanical strength and durability of textiles. They find their applications in fabric-based wearable heating devices, chemical sensors and energy storage. CNTs can improve the heating performance of dip-coated heating. CBNs screen-printed heaters on polyester cloth can also serve the same function with better performance. Moreover, carbon-based fabric sensors are suitable for the detection of such as acetone and methane vapor. Carbon compounds used on textile support have larger surface area and roughness resulting in higher charge accommodation. Plus, MWCNTs dip-coated supercapacitors on cotton fabric treated with CoCl2 solution show high electrochemical stability making them promising candidates for the applications in energy storage sector.
•
In short, CBNs come with bundles of merits. Their most prominent demerit is their inclination to agglomeration which can be solved through modification. In fact, modified CBNs show better performance in different applications compared to those shown by unmodified ones.

Fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, graphene nanoparticles, nanodiamonds, and carbon quantum dots are the primary nanomaterials derived from carbon, renowned for their unique properties that make them highly desirable for various technological applications. While they share many fields of application, this paper emphasizes the significance of using CBNs in biomedicine, catalysis, environmental remediation, and E-textiles. Despite their potential, carbon-based nanomaterials still face numerous research challenges that must be addressed to achieve scalability. Overcoming these obstacles represents a promising direction for CBNs development, likely to attract significant interest across multiple research fields. Consequently, we describe the main characteristics, properties, and applications of these carbon nanostructures, which are pivotal in advancing nanotechnology.
